\(\theta = -0.53\), with a p-value of 0.32. We fail to reject the null hypothesis. This implies that \(\beta_1\) and \(\beta_2\) perfectly offset each other; if both candidates double their budgets, the net effect on the election outcome is practically zero.

title: 4  Multiple Regression Analysis: Inference
text:
To study whether campaign expenditures affect election outcomes: voteA is the percentage of the vote received by Candidate A, expendA and expendB are campaign expenditures by Candidates A and B, and prtystrA is a measure of party strength for Candidate A \[ voteA = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \log(expendA) + \beta_2 \log(expendB) + \beta_3 prtystrA + u\] Interpret \(\beta_1\)











Tip



The Two Times Rule
If the absolute estimate is at least **twice as large** as its standard error, it is statistically significant




In terms of the parameters, state the null hypothesis that a 1% increase in A’s expenditures is offset by a 1% increase in B’s expenditures.






Estimate the given model using the data in VOTE1 and report the results in usual form. Do A’s expenditures affect the outcome? What about B’s expenditures? Can you use these results to test the hypothesis in part (ii)?







Estimate a model that directly gives the t statistic for testing the hypothesis in part (ii). What do you conclude? (Use a two-sided alternative.)

Moving down one spot in the law school rankings (e.g., from 10th to 11th) is associated with an estimated 0.3% reduction in median starting salary, holding all else constant.


Are features of the incoming class of students—namely, LSAT and GPA—individually or jointly significant for explaining salary?



joint_test <- linearHypothesis(model_lawschl85,
                               c("LSAT = 0", "GPA = 0"))

print(joint_test)


Linear hypothesis test:
LSAT = 0
GPA = 0

Model 1: restricted model
Model 2: lsalary ~ LSAT + GPA + llibvol + lcost + rank

  Res.Df    RSS Df Sum of Sq      F    Pr(>F)    
1    132 1.8942                                  
2    130 1.6427  2   0.25151 9.9518 9.518e-05 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1


Interpretation:
Individually, GPA is highly significant while LSAT is not (likely due to collinearity with school rank). However, the joint F-test yields a highly significant p-value, meaning we reject the null hypothesis; together, the incoming class characteristics are jointly significant.


Test whether the size of the entering class (clsize) or the size of the faculty (faculty) needs to be added to this equation; carry out a single test



model_unrestricted <- lm(
  lsalary ~ LSAT + GPA + llibvol + lcost + rank + clsize + faculty, 
  data = df_lawsch85
)

joint_test <- linearHypothesis(model_unrestricted, c("clsize = 0", "faculty = 0"))
print(joint_test)


Linear hypothesis test:
clsize = 0
faculty = 0

Model 1: restricted model
Model 2: lsalary ~ LSAT + GPA + llibvol + lcost + rank + clsize + faculty

  Res.Df    RSS Df Sum of Sq      F Pr(>F)
1    125 1.5974                           
2    123 1.5732  2  0.024259 0.9484 0.3902


Interpretation:
Restricted model shows no significant difference from unrestricted model (p = 0.39). We fail to reject the null hypothesis, class size and faculty provide no additional explanatory power


What factors might influence the rank of the law school that are not included in the salary regression?


Interpretation:
Unobserved factors absorbed by the rank variable might include the school’s geographic location, the strength of its alumni network, and the socioeconomic background of the student body (such as family income or father’s occupation)

The Easy Way

deltaMethod(model_hprice1, "150 * sqrft + bdrms")

                    Estimate       SE    2.5 % 97.5 %
150 * sqrft + bdrms 0.085801 0.026768 0.033338 0.1383


Interpretation:
Adding a 150 square foot bedroom is associated with an estimated 8.58% increase in price. We are 95% sure the true value lies in the interval [3.33%, 13.83%]


write \(\beta_2\) in terms of \(\theta\) and \(\beta_1\)


The Math Way:
To get the confidence Interval for a 150 square foot bedroom, let \(\theta = 150 \times \beta_1 + \beta_2\).
\[
\begin{align}
\beta_2 &= \theta - 150 \beta_1\\
\log(price) &= \beta_0 + \beta_1(sqrft) + (\theta - 150 \beta_1)bdrms + u \\
\log(price) &= \beta_0 + \beta_1 (sqrft - 150 \cdot bdrms) + \theta(bdrms) + u
\end{align}
\]


Obtain standard error for \(\hat \theta\) and construct a 95% confidence Interval


We run regression to obtain its standard error and confidence interval

df_hprice1$sqrft_adj <- df_hprice1$sqrft - 150 * df_hprice1$bdrms

model_theta <- lm(I(100*lprice) ~ sqrft_adj + bdrms, data = df_hprice1)




cm <- c("bdrms" = "Effect of 150 sqft Bedroom (%)")

modelsummary(
  model_theta,
  coef_map = cm,
  fmt = 2,
  estimate = "{estimate} [{conf.low}, {conf.high}]",
  statistic = NULL,   
  title = "Estimated Impact of Adding a 150 sqft Bedroom",
  gof_omit = ".*"     
)



    

    

    
    
    
      
        Estimated Impact of Adding a 150 sqft Bedroom
        
              
                 
                (1)
              
        
        
        
                
                  Effect of 150 sqft Bedroom (%)
                  8.58 [3.26, 13.90]
                
        
      
    



Interpretation:
We scaled dependent variable by 100 to get percentage values for the log-level model. The estimated effect is 8.58% with a confidence interval of [3.26%, 13.90%].






Caution



deltMethod relies on asymptotic theory, confidence intervals are calculated using Z distribution. Critical value = 1.96
lm uses t distribution. With 85 degrees of freedom, critical value = 1.988

4.6 C6 [WAGE2]

Use the data in WAGE2 for this exercise.



Consider the standard wage equation \[\log(wage) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 educ + \beta_2 exper + \beta_3 tenure + u\] State the null hypothesis that another year of general workforce experience has the same effect on log(wage) as another year of tenure with the current employer.




Test the null hypothesis in part (i) against a two-sided alternative, at the 5% significance level, by constructing a 95% confidence interval. What do you conclude?

text:
4.7 C7 [TWOYEAR]

Refer to the example used in Section 4-4. You will use the data set TWOYEAR.



The variable phsrank is the person’s high school percentile rank. (A higher number is better. For example, 90 means you are ranked better than 90 percent of your graduating class.) Find the smallest, largest, and average phsrank in the sample.




Add phsrank to equation (4.26) and report the OLS estimates in the usual form. Is phsrank statistically significant? How much is 10 percentage points of high school rank worth in terms of wage?




Does adding phsrank to (4.26) substantively change the conclusions on the returns to twoand four-year colleges? Explain.




The data set contains a variable called id. Explain why if you add id to equation (4.17) or (4.26) you expect it to be statistically insignificant. What is the two-sided p-value?

title: 4  Multiple Regression Analysis: Inference
text:
4.8 C8 [401KSUBS]

The data set 401KSUBS contains information on net financial wealth (nettfa), age of the survey respondent (age), annual family income (inc), family size (fsize), and participation in certain pension plans for people in the United States. The wealth and income variables are both recorded in thousands of dollars. For this question, use only the data for single-person households (so fsize = 1).



How many single-person households are there in the data set?




Use OLS to estimate the model \[nettfa = \beta_0 + \beta_1 inc + \beta_2 age + u\] and report the results using the usual format. Be sure to use only the single-person households in the sample. Interpret the slope coefficients. Are there any surprises in the slope estimates?




Does the intercept from the regression in part (ii) have an interesting meaning? Explain.




Find the p-value for the test \(H_0: \beta_2 = 1\) against \(H_1: \beta_2 < 1\). Do you reject \(H_0\) at the 1% significance level?




If you do a simple regression of nettfa on inc, is the estimated coefficient on inc much different from the estimate in part (ii)? Why or why not?

4.10 C9 [DISCRIM]

Use OLS to estimate the model \[log(psoda) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 prpblck + \beta_2 log(income) + \beta_3 prppov + u,\] and report the results in the usual form. Is \(\hat{\beta}_1\) statistically different from zero at the 5% level against a two-sided alternative? What about at the 1% level?




What is the correlation between log(income) and prppov? Is each variable statistically significant in any case? Report the two-sided \(p\)-values.




To the regression in part (i), add the variable log(hseval). Interpret its coefficient and report the two-sided \(p\)-value for \(H_0: \beta_{log(hseval)} = 0\).




In the regression in part (iii), what happens to the individual statistical significance of log(income) and prppov? Are these variables jointly significant? (Compute a \(p\)-value.) What do you make of your answers?




Given the results of the previous regressions, which one would you report as most reliable in determining whether the racial makeup of a zip code influences local fast-food prices?

Add the variables lenrol and lstaff to the regression from part (i). What happens to the coefficient on bs? How does the situation compare with that in Table 4.1?




Why is the standard error on the bs coefficient smaller in part (ii) than in part (i)? (Hint: What happens to the error variance versus multicollinearity when lenrol and lstaff are added?)




How come the coefficient on lstaff is negative? Is it large in magnitude?

model_wage1 <- lm(
  wage ~ educ + exper + tenure,
  data = df_wage1
)

ggplot(data = model_wage1, aes(x = .resid)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30)

Warning: `fortify(<lm>)` was deprecated in ggplot2 4.0.0.

The residuals are highly right-skewed and clearly violate the assumption of normality.
model_lwage <- lm(
  lwage ~ educ + exper + tenure,
  data = df_wage1

  geom_histogram(bins = 30)
Interpretation:
Residuals of the log wage model seem symmetric with few outliers.
shapiro_level <- shapiro.test(resid(model_wage1))
print(shapiro_level)


    Shapiro-Wilk normality test

data:  resid(model_wage1)
W = 0.89317, p-value < 2.2e-16

shapiro_log <- shapiro.test(resid(model_lwage))
print(shapiro_log)


    Shapiro-Wilk normality test

data:  resid(model_lwage)
W = 0.98946, p-value = 0.000787
Interpretation:
The log-level model is much closer to satisfying MLR.6. The level-level residuals are heavily right-skewed, blatantly violating the normality assumption. By taking the log of wages, the distribution of residuals becomes roughly symmetric and bell-shaped, which aligns much more closely with the normality requirement of MLR.6.

  pull(std.error)

print(se_sub / se_full)

[1] 1.307676


Interpretation:
The ratio is approximately 1.3. This is close to the theoretical penalty of \(\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414\). The penalty is not exactly 1.414 because while subsetting the data cut \(SST_j\) roughly in half, it also caused slight fluctuations in the residual variance (\(\hat{\sigma}\)) and the variable’s collinearity (\(R^2_j\)). \[SE(\hat \beta_j) = \frac{\hat \sigma}{\sqrt{SST_j(1-R^2_j)}}\]

compute the LM statistic for testing whether motheduc and fatheduc are jointly significant. \[\begin{align}bwght =& \beta_0 + \beta_1 cigs + \beta_2 parity + \beta_3 faminc \\&+ \beta_4 motheduc + \beta_5 fatheduc + u\end{align}\]

The Math Way:
To do Lagrange Multiplier Test:

Filter the data from missing data
Estimate the restricted model
Extract the residuals \(\tilde u\)
Regress \(\tilde u\) on all independent variables
Extract \(R^2_u\) and \(n\)
\(LM = n \cdot R^2_u\)
\(LM \sim \chi^2_q\) where q is number of restrictions


df_clean <- df_bwght |>
  drop_na(bwght, cigs, parity, faminc, motheduc, fatheduc)

model_r <- lm(bwght ~ cigs + parity + faminc,
  data = df_clean
)

df_clean$u_tilde <- resid(model_r)

model_aux <- lm(
  u_tilde ~ cigs + parity + faminc + motheduc + fatheduc,
  data = df_clean
)

n <- nobs(model_aux)
r2_aux <- summary(model_aux)$r.squared

lm_stat <- n * r2_aux
p_val <- pchisq(lm_stat, df = 2, lower.tail = FALSE)

cat("LM Statistic: ", lm_stat, "\n")

LM Statistic:  2.882101 

cat("P-value:      ", p_val, "\n")

P-value:       0.236679 


Interpretation:
Calculated LM Statistic is 2.8 with p value 0.24. We fail to reject the null hypothesis; education is not jointly statistically significant.






Important



If we wanted to test if education = 0.5 not just zero, we will modify y from step two
df_clean <- df_clean |>  mutate(bwght_tilde = bwght - (0.5 * motheduc) - (0.5 * fatheduc))

5  Multiple Regression Analysis: OLS Asymptotics

title: 5  Multiple Regression Analysis: OLS Asymptotics
text:
5.4 C4 [SKEWNESS]

Several statistics are commonly used to detect nonnormality in underlying population distributions. Here we will study one that measures the amount of skewness in a distribution. Recall that any normally distributed random variable is symmetric about its mean; therefore, if we standardize a symmetrically distributed random variable, say \(z = (y - \mu_y)/\sigma_y\), where \(\mu_y = \text{E}(y)\) and \(\sigma_y = \text{sd}(y)\), then \(z\) has mean zero, variance one, and \(\text{E}(z^3) = 0\). Given a sample of data \(\{y_i: i = 1, \dots, n\}\), we can standardize \(y_i\) in the sample by using \(z_i = (y_i - \hat{\mu}_y)/\hat{\sigma}_y\), where \(\hat{\mu}_y\) is the sample mean and \(\hat{\sigma}_y\) is the sample standard deviation. (We ignore the fact that these are estimates based on the sample.) A sample statistic that measures skewness is \(n^{-1} \sum_{i=1}^n z_i^3\), or where \(n\) is replaced with \((n - 1)\) as a degrees-of-freedom adjustment. If \(y\) has a normal distribution in the population, the skewness measure in the sample for the standardized values should not differ significantly from zero.



First use the data set 401KSUBS, keeping only observations with fsize = 1. Find the skewness measure for inc. Do the same for log(inc). Which variable has more skewness and therefore seems less likely to be normally distributed?




Next use BWGHT2. Find the skewness measures for bwght and log(bwght). What do you conclude?




Evaluate the following statement: “The logarithmic transformation always makes a positive variable look more nearly normally distributed.”




If we are interested in the normality assumption in the context of regression, should we be evaluating the unconditional distributions of \(y\) and \(\log(y)\)? Explain.
crumbs: PART 1: Regression Analysis with Cross-Sectional Data

title: 5  Multiple Regression Analysis: OLS Asymptotics

5.6 C6 [ECONMATH]

Use the data in ECONMATH to answer this question. (i) Logically, what are the smallest and largest values that can be taken on by the variable score? What are the smallest and largest values in the sample? (ii) Consider the linear model \[score = \beta_0 + \beta_1 colgpa + \beta_2 actmth + \beta_3 acteng + u.\] Why can Assumption MLR.6 not hold for the error term \(u\)? What consequences does this have for using the usual \(t\) statistic to test H\(_0\): \(\beta_3 = 0\)? (iii) Estimate the model from part (ii) and obtain the \(t\) statistic and associated \(p\)-value for testing H\(_0\): \(\beta_3 = 0\). How would you defend your findings to someone who makes the following statement: “You cannot trust that \(p\)-value because clearly the error term in the equation cannot have a normal distribution.”

title: 5  Multiple Regression Analysis: OLS Asymptotics
text:
5.7 C7 [APPLE]

Use the data in APPLE to answer the following questions.



Estimate the following equation by OLS, and report the results in the usual format, including standard errors, sample size, and \(R\)-squared: \[ecolbs = \beta_0 + \beta_1 ecoprc + \beta_2 regprc + \beta_3 faminc + \beta_4 age + \beta_5 educ + u\] Are any of the coefficients on faminc, age, and educ individually statistically significant? Do any seem practically large?




Obtain the \(F\) statistic for joint significance of faminc, age, and educ; report its value, and the associated \(p\)-value, to three decimal places. What do you conclude?




Estimate the equation without the variables faminc, age, and educ. Obtain the residuals, \(\tilde{u}_i\). If you regress these residuals on \(ecoprc_i\) and \(regprc_i\), what is the \(R\)-squared, and why?




Now use the residuals from part (iii) to obtain the \(LM\) statistic for joint significance of faminc, age, and educ. Report the statistic to three decimal places, and likewise with the associated \(p\)-value. How similar is your conclusion to when you use the \(F\) statistic?

Interpretation:
In the multiple regression model, the coefficient for distance to the incinerator (\(\beta_1\)) shrinks considerably and becomes statistically insignificant. The large, significant estimate observed in the simple model was heavily driven by omitted variable bias; distance from the incinerator is positively correlated with other desirable, price-increasing characteristics, such as larger lot sizes and closer proximity to the interstate. By including these controls, the isolated effect of the incinerator is revealed to be much smaller. Furthermore, the fact that these newly added spatial variables also lack individual statistical significance strongly suggests multicollinearity. The model struggles to disentangle the individual marginal effects of these highly correlated geographic features, resulting in inflated standard errors.

Interpretation:
By adding the squared term for the distance to the interstate, we corrected a functional form misspecification present in the previous model. Both the linear term (2.07) and the squared term (-0.12) are statistically significant at the 1% level. The negative coefficient on the squared term indicates an inverted U-shaped relationship: moving away from the interstate initially increases house prices (escaping the noise penalty), but eventually, the marginal effect becomes negative as the house becomes too isolated (the commute penalty).With the non-linear spatial effect of the interstate properly modeled, the isolated effect of the incinerator is clarified. The coefficient \(\beta_1 = 0.19\) is statistically significant (\(p < 0.01\)). Holding all other controlled factors constant, a 1% increase in distance from the incinerator is associated with a 0.19% increase in the housing price.






Note



Optimal distance from interstate is obtained by solving
\[
\frac{\partial \log(price)}{\partial \log(intst)} = 2.07 - 2(0.12) \log(intst) = 0
\]
Which can be solved easily using the formula
\[
x^* = \left| \dfrac{\hat \beta_1}{2 \hat \beta_2}\right| = \dfrac{2.07}{0.24} = 8.625
\]
Where \(x^*\) is in log terms, so \(\exp{x^*} =  \exp(8.625) = 5569\)

Adding the squared term for the distance to the incinerator causes both the linear (\(\beta_1 = 0.87\), \(p = 0.67\)) and quadratic (\(-0.04\), \(p = 0.74\)) terms to become statistically insignificant. This insignificance is driven by severe variance inflation due to structural multicollinearity between \(\log(dist)\) and \([\log(dist)]^2\). We conclude that there is no evidence of a non-linear relationship between house prices and proximity to the incinerator. The strictly linear functional form for \(\log(dist)\) established in part (iii) is the correct specification.

Is \(exper^2\) Statistically significant at the 1% level?


Interpretation: Yes, although the estimated effect is \(-0.001\) which is almost zero, the standard error is microscopic.






Important



Think of what experience squared represents, a 30 years of experience will be 900 years.




Find the approximate return to the fifth year of experience, and 20 \[
\% \Delta \widehat{wage} \approx 100(\hat \beta_2 + 2 \hat \beta_3 exper) \Delta exper
\]


The Math Way: \[\begin{align}
100(0.041 + 2(-0.001)\cdot 4) &= 3.53\%\\
100(0.041 + 2(-0.001) \cdot 19 &= 1.3\%)
\end{align}\]
The Code Way:

compare_df <- data.frame(
  exper = c(4, 5),
  educ = 12
)

log_wages <- predict(model_c2, newdata = compare_df)
log_diff <- log_wages[2] - log_wages[1]

pct_change_exact <- 100 * (exp(log_diff) - 1)
pct_change_approx <- 100 * log_diff

paste(pct_change_approx, pct_change_exact)

[1] "3.45868520562578 3.51919330198605"








Note



Why do my returns differ (3.53% vs. 3.45% vs. 3.52%)?
The discrepancy comes from how we handle the “curvature” of the Experience Squared term:

The Math Way (3.53%): Uses the derivative (\(100 \cdot [\beta_2 + 2\beta_3 \cdot 4]\)). This is a linear approximation. It assumes the slope is a flat line at exactly Year 4. It ignores the “bend” that happens as you move toward Year 5.
Approx Predict (3.46%): This is the Log Difference (\(\hat{y}_5 - \hat{y}_4\)). It accounts for the curve between the two years but stays in “log points.”
Exact Predict (3.52%): This is the Exponential Change (\(100 \cdot [e^{(\hat{y}_5 - \hat{y}_4)} - 1]\)). This is the only mathematically “perfect” representation of the percentage increase in actual dollars.



Interpretation:
The estimated return to experience is positive but diminishing. Obtaining a fifth year of experience (moving from 4 to 5 years) is associated with an expected wage increase of approximately 3.5%, holding other factors constant. However, by the twentieth year of experience (moving from 19 to 20 years), the marginal return drops to approximately 1.3%


At what value of experience does additional experience lower predicted log(wage)


\[
\begin{align}
\% \Delta \widehat{wage} &\approx 100(\hat \beta_2 + 2 \hat \beta_3 exper) \Delta exper \\
0 &= 100(\hat \beta_2 + 2 \hat \beta_3 exper) \Delta exper\\
0 &= \hat \beta_2 + 2 \hat \beta_3 exper\\
2 \hat \beta_3 exper &= - \hat \beta_2\\
exper &= \dfrac{- \hat \beta_2}{2 \hat \beta_3}\\
exper &= \dfrac{-0.04101}{2(-0.00071)} \\
&\approx 28.88 \text{ years}
\end{align}
\]

df_wage1 |>
  summarise(
    n_declining = sum(exper >= 29),
    pct_declining = mean(exper >= 29) * 100
  ) |>
  round(2)

  n_declining pct_declining
1         121            23


Interpretation:
The optimal amount of experience for maximizing wages is approximately 29 years. Then the return becomes negative. The sample includes 121 workers with declining return of experience. These workers account for 23% of the workers in the sample.
crumbs: PART 1: Regression Analysis with Cross-Sectional Data
6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues

title: 6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues
text:
6.2 C3


Show that the return to another year of education (in decimal form), holding exper fixed, is (_1 + _3 exper).


The partial derivative of ((wage)) with respect to (educ) is ( = _1 + _3 exper). Since ((wage)) is the natural log, the change in ((wage)) approximates the proportional change in (wage) for a small change in (educ). Therefore, holding (exper) fixed, the return to an additional year of education (in decimal form) is (_1 + _3 exper).


State the null hypothesis that the return on education does not depend on the level of exper. What do you think is the appropriate alternative?


The null hypothesis is that the return to education does not depend on (exper), i.e., the coefficient on the interaction term (educ exper) is zero:
(H_0: _3 = 0).
The appropriate alternative is that the return to education does depend on (exper), i.e., (H_1: _3 ). This is a two-sided alternative.


Use the data in WAGE2 to test the null hypothesis in (ii) against your stated alternative.


(This part requires running the regression using the WAGE2 dataset. The test involves estimating ((wage) = _0 + _1 educ + _2 exper + _3 educ exper + u) and then testing (H_0: _3 = 0) using a (t)-test. The result would depend on the estimated coefficient and its standard error. Without the data, I cannot provide the numerical result.)


Let (_1) denote the return on education (in decimal form), when (exper = 10): (_1 = _1 + 10_3). Obtain (_1) and a 95% confidence interval for (_1).
(Hint: Write (_1 = _1 - 10_3) and plug this into the equation; then rearrange. This gives the regression for obtaining the confidence interval for (_1).)


Substitute (_1 = _1 - 10_3) into the model:
[ (wage) = _0 + (_1 - 10_3) educ + _2 exper + _3 educ exper + u ]
[ (wage) = _0 + _1 educ + _2 exper + _3 (educ exper - 10 educ) + u ]
[ (wage) = _0 + _1 educ + _2 exper + _3 [educ (exper - 10)] + u ]
Run the regression of ((wage)) on (educ), (exper), and (educ (exper - 10)). The coefficient on (educ) gives (_1), and its confidence interval is the desired interval.
crumbs: PART 1: Regression Analysis with Cross-Sectional Data
6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues

title: 6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues
text:
6.3 C4

Use the data in GPA2 for this exercise.



Estimate the model (sat = _0 + _1 hsize + _2 hsize^2 + u), where (hsize) is the size of the graduating class (in hundreds), and write the results in the usual form. Is the quadratic term statistically significant?


Estimate the regression using OLS. The results take the form:
[ = _0 + _1 hsize + _2 hsize^2 ]
with standard errors in parentheses. Test (H_0: _2 = 0) using a (t)-test. If the (p)-value is less than the significance level (e.g., 0.05), then the quadratic term is statistically significant.


Using the estimated equation from part (i), what is the “optimal” high school size? Justify your answer.


The optimal (hsize) is found by taking the derivative of () with respect to (hsize) and setting it to zero:
[ = _1 + 2_2 hsize = 0 ]
Solving gives (hsize^* = -_1 / (2_2)). Multiply by 100 to get the optimal class size in students.


Is this analysis representative of the academic performance of all high school seniors? Explain.


Not necessarily. The data likely come from a specific sample, and the relationship may not hold for all high school seniors. Additionally, the model only controls for (hsize) and (hsize^2), omitting other factors that affect SAT scores.


Find the estimated optimal high school size, using ((sat)) as the dependent variable. Is it much different from what you obtained in part (ii)?


Estimate ((sat) = _0 + _1 hsize + _2 hsize^2 + u). The optimal (hsize) is again (hsize^* = -_1 / (2_2)). Compare this value to the one from part (ii). Without the data, I cannot provide numerical differences.
crumbs: PART 1: Regression Analysis with Cross-Sectional Data
6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues

title: 6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues
text:
6.4 C5

Use the housing price data in HPRICE1 for this exercise.



Estimate the model ((price) = _0 + _1 (lotsize) + _2 (sqrft) + _3 bdrms + u) and report the results in the usual OLS format.


Estimate the regression using OLS. The results take the form:
[ = _0 + _1 (lotsize) + _2 (sqrft) + _3 bdrms ]
with standard errors in parentheses.


Find the predicted value of ((price)), when (lotsize = 20,000), (sqrft = 2,500), and (bdrms = 4). Using the methods in Section 6-4, find the predicted value of price at the same values of the explanatory variables.


Compute () by plugging in the values:
((20000) ), ((2500) ), and (bdrms = 4).
Then ( = ()).


For explaining variation in (price), decide whether you prefer the model from part (i) or the model (price = _0 + _1 lotsize + _2 sqrft + _3 bdrms + u).


Compare (R^2) or adjusted (R^2) between the two models. The log-linear model (part i) often fits better when the dependent variable has a skewed distribution. Also, consider economic interpretation and residual diagnostics.
crumbs: PART 1: Regression Analysis with Cross-Sectional Data
6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues

title: 6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues
text:
6.5 C6

Use the data in VOTE1 for this exercise.



Consider a model with an interaction between expenditures:
(voteA = _0 + _1 prtystrA + _2 expendA + _3 expendB + _4 expendA expendB + u).
What is the partial effect of (expendB) on (voteA), holding (prtystrA) and (expendA) fixed? What is the partial effect of (expendA) on (voteA)? Is the expected sign for (_4) obvious?


Partial effect of (expendB): ( = _3 + _4 expendA).
Partial effect of (expendA): ( = _2 + _4 expendB).
The expected sign for (_4) is ambiguous: an increase in one candidate’s spending could have diminishing returns when the other spends heavily, suggesting (_4 < 0).


Estimate the equation in part (i) and report the results in the usual form. Is the interaction term statistically significant?


Estimate the regression using OLS. Test (H_0: _4 = 0) using a (t)-test. Without data, I cannot provide the result.


Find the average of (expendA) in the sample. Fix (expendA) at 300 (for $300,000). What is the estimated effect of another $100,000 spent by Candidate B on (voteA)? Is this a large effect?


The effect of a $100,000 increase in (expendB) is approximately (100 (_3 + _4 )). Whether it is large depends on the magnitude relative to the variation in (voteA).


Now fix (expendB) at 100. What is the estimated effect of (expendA = 100) on (voteA)? Does this make sense?


The effect is (100 (_2 + _4 )). This should be positive if spending increases vote share, but may be smaller if interaction is negative.


Now, estimate a model that replaces the interaction with (shareA), Candidate A’s percentage share of total campaign expenditures. Does it make sense to hold both (expendA) and (expendB) constant?


Holding both (expendA) and (expendB) constant while changing (shareA) is impossible because (shareA = ). If both are fixed, (shareA) cannot change. So the model with (shareA) is more appropriate for capturing relative spending effects.
crumbs: PART 1: Regression Analysis with Cross-Sectional Data
6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues

title: 6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues
text:
6.6 C6 (continued)


(Requires calculus) In the model from part (v), find the partial effect of expendB on voteA, holding prtystrA and expendA fixed. Evaluate this at expendA = 300 and expendB = 0 and comment on the results.


The model with shareA is: [ voteA = _0 + _1 prtystrA + _2 shareA + u ] where ( shareA = ).
The partial effect of expendB on voteA, holding expendA fixed, is found using the chain rule: [ = _2 = _2 ( - ) ]
At ( expendA = 300 ) and ( expendB = 0 ): [ = _2 ( - ) = _2 ( - ) ]
Since (_2) is expected to be positive (higher share of spending increases vote share), the partial effect is negative. This means that when Candidate B has zero spending, an initial increase in B’s spending reduces A’s vote share because it lowers A’s share of total spending. This makes intuitive sense: even a small amount of spending by B reduces A’s percentage of total expenditures, which hurts A.
crumbs: PART 1: Regression Analysis with Cross-Sectional Data
6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues

title: 6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues
text:
6.7 C7

Use the data in ATTEND for this exercise.



In the model of Example 6.3, argue that [ stdnfl / prigPA _2 + 2_4 prigPA + _6 atndrte. ] Use equation (6.19) to estimate the partial effect when (prigPA = 2.59) and (atndrte = 82). Interpret your estimate.


From Example 6.3, the model is: [ stndfnl = _0 + _1 atndrte + _2 priGPA + _3 ACT + _4 priGPA^2 + _5 ACT^2 + _6 priGPA atndrte + u ]
Taking the partial derivative with respect to (priGPA): [ = _2 + 2_4 priGPA + _6 atndrte ]
Using the estimates from equation (6.19) in the text (I’ll denote them symbolically): [ = _2 + 2_4 (2.59) + _6 (82) ]
If (_2) is positive, (_4) is negative, and (_6) is positive, the net effect at these values can be computed. The interpretation is the estimated change in final exam score (in standard deviations) for a one-unit increase in prior college GPA, holding attendance rate fixed at 82%.


Show that the equation can be written as [ stndfnl = _0 + _1 atndrte + _2 priGPA + _3 ACT + _4 (priGPA - 2.59)^2 + _5 ACT^2 + _6 priGPA (atndrte - 82) + u, ] where (_2 = _2 + 2_4 (2.59) + _6 (82)). Use this to obtain the standard error of (_2) from part (i).


Starting from the original model, expand and group terms to center (priGPA) at 2.59 and (atndrte) at 82. The transformation yields: - The coefficient on (atndrte) remains (_1) - The coefficient on (priGPA) becomes (_2 = _2 + 2_4(2.59) + _6(82)) - The term (_4 (priGPA - 2.59)^2) captures the quadratic effect - The term (_6 priGPA (atndrte - 82)) captures the interaction with centered attendance
In this reparameterized regression, the standard error of (_2) is directly reported as the standard error of the coefficient on (priGPA). This provides the standard error for the partial effect estimated in part (i).


Suppose that, in place of (priGPA (atndrte - 82)), you put ((priGPA - 2.59) (atndrte - 82)). Now how do you interpret the coefficients on (atndrte) and (priGPA)?


If the interaction term is ((priGPA - 2.59)(atndrte - 82)), then: - The coefficient on (atndrte) becomes the partial effect of attendance when (priGPA = 2.59) - The coefficient on (priGPA) becomes the partial effect of prior GPA when (atndrte = 82)
Both coefficients now have direct interpretations at the centering values, which are often chosen as sample means or other relevant reference points.
crumbs: PART 1: Regression Analysis with Cross-Sectional Data
6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues

title: 6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues
text:
6.8 C8

Use the data in HPRICE1 for this exercise.



Estimate the model [ price = _0 + _1 lotsize + _2 sqrft + _3 bdrms + u ] and report the results in the usual form, including the standard error of the regression. Obtain predicted price, when we plug in (lotsize = 10,000), (sqrft = 2,300), and (bdrms = 4); round this price to the nearest dollar.


Run the OLS regression. The results take the form: [ = _0 + _1 lotsize + _2 sqrft + _3 bdrms ] with standard errors in parentheses, and report () (SER).
Predicted price: [ _0 = _0 + _1 (10000) + _2 (2300) + _3 (4) ] Round to nearest dollar.


Run a regression that allows you to put a 95% confidence interval around the predicted value in part (i). Note that your prediction will differ somewhat due to rounding error.


To obtain a confidence interval for the expected price (i.e., the mean price for houses with those characteristics), create a new variable that equals 1 for the observation with the desired characteristics, or use the predict command with the stdp option (in Stata) to get the standard error of the predicted mean. The 95% CI is (0 t{0.025, n-k-1} se(_0)).


Let (price) be the unknown future selling price of the house with the characteristics used in parts (i) and (ii). Find a 95% CI for (price) and comment on the width of this confidence interval.


A 95% prediction interval for an individual house price is: [ 0 t{0.025, n-k-1} ] This interval is wider than the confidence interval for the mean because it accounts for both estimation error and the inherent variability of individual outcomes. The prediction interval reflects the uncertainty in forecasting a single new observation.
crumbs: PART 1: Regression Analysis with Cross-Sectional Data
6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues

title: 6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues
text:
6.9 C9

Use the data in NBASAL for this exercise.



Estimate a model relating points-per-game (points) to years in the league (exper), age, and years played in college (coll). Include a quadratic in exper; the other variables should appear in level form. Report the results in the usual way.


Estimate: [ points = _0 + _1 exper + _2 exper^2 + _3 age + _4 coll + u ] Report coefficients, standard errors, (R^2), and sample size.


Holding college years and age fixed, at what value of experience does the next year of experience actually reduce points-per-game? Does this make sense?


The effect of experience is: [ = _1 + 2_2 exper ] Set equal to zero: (exper^* = -_1 / (2_2)). If (_1 > 0) and (_2 < 0), this gives a turning point. After this point, additional experience reduces scoring. This could make sense if players decline with age or if the most productive years occur early in a career.


Why do you think coll has a negative and statistically significant coefficient? (Hint: NBA players can be drafted before finishing their college careers and even directly out of high school.)


Players who leave college early (or skip college) are often the most talented prospects. Thus, coll is negatively correlated with unobserved ability. Conditional on current performance (points), having spent more years in college may indicate lower innate talent, leading to a negative coefficient.


Add a quadratic in age to the equation. Is it needed? What does this appear to imply about the effects of age, once experience and education are controlled for?


Add (age^2) and test its significance. If (age^2) is statistically significant, it suggests a nonlinear age profile. Since exper and age are highly correlated, including both quadratics may cause multicollinearity. The result might imply that age has little additional effect beyond experience once both are flexibly controlled for.


Now regress ((wage)) on points, exper, (exper^2), age, and coll. Report the results in the usual format.


Estimate: [ (wage) = _0 + _1 points + _2 exper + _3 exper^2 + _4 age + _5 coll + u ] Report coefficients, standard errors, (R^2).


Test whether age and coll are jointly significant in the regression from part (v). What does this imply about whether age and education have separate effects on wage, once productivity and seniority are accounted for?


Test (H_0: _4 = 0, _5 = 0) using an (F)-test. If they are jointly insignificant, it suggests that once current productivity (points) and experience (exper) are controlled for, age and college years do not independently affect wages. This would imply that NBA salaries are determined primarily by current performance and seniority, not by age or education per se.
crumbs: PART 1: Regression Analysis with Cross-Sectional Data
6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues

title: 6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues
text:
6.10 C10

Use the data in BWGHT2 for this exercise.



Estimate the equation [ (bwght) = _0 + _1 npvis + _2 npvis^2 + u ] by OLS, and report the results in the usual way. Is the quadratic term significant?


Estimate the regression. The results take the form: [ = _0 + _1 npvis + _2 npvis^2 ] with standard errors in parentheses. Test (H_0: _2 = 0) using a (t)-test. If the (p)-value is less than 0.05, the quadratic term is statistically significant.


Show that, based on the equation from part (i), the number of prenatal visits that maximizes ((bwght)) is estimated to be about 22. How many women had at least 22 prenatal visits in the sample?


The maximizing value is found by setting the derivative to zero: [ = _1 + 2_2 npvis = 0 npvis^* = -_1 / (2_2) ] If (_1 > 0) and (_2 < 0), this yields a positive optimum. The claim that (npvis^* ) suggests the estimated coefficients satisfy this relationship. Count the number of women in the sample with (npvis ).


Does it make sense that birth weight is actually predicted to decline after 22 prenatal visits? Explain.


It may not make sense biologically—more prenatal care should not reduce birth weight. The decline after 22 visits likely reflects that women with complications receive more visits, creating a selection bias. The quadratic captures a correlation, not a causal declining effect.


Add mother’s age to the equation, using a quadratic functional form. Holding (npvis) fixed, at what mother’s age is the birth weight of the child maximized? What fraction of women in the sample are older than the “optimal” age?


Estimate: [ (bwght) = _0 + _1 npvis + _2 npvis^2 + _3 age + _4 age^2 + u ] The optimal age (holding (npvis) fixed) is (age^* = -_3 / (2_4)), assuming (_4 < 0). Compute the fraction of women with (age > age^*).


Would you say that mother’s age and number of prenatal visits explain a lot of the variation in ((bwght))?


Look at the (R^2) from the model in part (iv). If (R^2) is small (e.g., below 0.10), these variables explain little variation in birth weight, which is typical as birth weight depends on many unobserved factors.


Using quadratics for both (npvis) and (age), decide whether using the natural log or the level of (bwght) is better for predicting (bwght).


Compare (R^2) from the model with ((bwght)) as dependent variable versus a model with (bwght) as dependent variable (using the same right-hand side variables). Since the dependent variables differ, compare adjusted (R^2) or use a Box-Cox test. Alternatively, compare predicted values and residual diagnostics; the log model often works better for right-skewed outcomes like birth weight.
crumbs: PART 1: Regression Analysis with Cross-Sectional Data
6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues

title: 6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues
text:
6.11 C11

Use APPLE to verify some of the claims made in Section 6-3.



Run the regression (ecolbs) on (ecoprc), (regprc) and report the results in the usual form, including the (R)-squared and adjusted (R)-squared. Interpret the coefficients on the price variables and comment on their signs and magnitudes.


Estimate: [ = _0 + _1 ecoprc + _2 regprc ] Expected signs: (_1 < 0) (own price effect), (_2 > 0) (cross-price effect—substitute good). Report (R^2) and adjusted (R^2).


Are the price variables statistically significant? Report the (p)-values for the individual (t) tests.


Compute (p)-values for (H_0: _1 = 0) and (H_0: _2 = 0). Typically both are significant at conventional levels.


What is the range of fitted values for (ecolbs)? What fraction of the sample reports (ecolbs = 0)? Comment.


Compute min and max of (). Many respondents may report zero demand, so fitted values could be negative—a common issue with linear probability-like models for demand with censoring. Report the fraction with (ecolbs = 0).


Do you think the price variables together do a good job of explaining variation in (ecolbs)? Explain.


The (R^2) is likely low, as demand for a new product depends on many factors beyond prices. Low (R^2) does not imply bias, only that much variation remains unexplained.


Add the variables (faminc), (hhsize) (household size), (educ), and (age) to the regression from part (i). Find the (p)-value for their joint significance. What do you conclude?


Estimate the expanded model and perform an (F)-test of (H_0): coefficients on (faminc, hhsize, educ, age) are all zero. If the (p)-value is small, these variables jointly matter. Report the (p)-value.


Run separate simple regressions of (ecolbs) on (ecoprc) and then (ecolbs) on (regprc). How do the simple regression coefficients compare with the multiple regression from part (i)? Find the correlation coefficient between (ecoprc) and (regprc) to help explain your findings.


In simple regressions, the coefficients may be biased due to omitted variable bias. Since (ecoprc) and (regprc) are positively correlated (both prices tend to move together), the simple regression coefficient on (ecoprc) will be biased toward zero (if the cross-price effect is positive) or away (depending on signs). Compute the correlation to assess multicollinearity.


Consider a model that adds family income and the quantity demanded for regular apples: [ ecolbs = _0 + _1 ecoprc + _2 regprc + _3 faminc + _4 reglbs + u. ] From basic economic theory, which explanatory variable does not belong to the equation? When you drop the variables one at a time, do the sizes of the adjusted (R)-squareds affect your answer?


Economic theory says demand depends on prices and income, not on quantity demanded of another good (which is endogenous). (reglbs) does not belong; it is determined simultaneously with (ecolbs). Dropping (reglbs) should increase adjusted (R^2) if it is irrelevant, but if it is correlated with omitted factors, comparing adjusted (R^2) alone is not sufficient for model selection—endogeneity concerns dominate.
crumbs: PART 1: Regression Analysis with Cross-Sectional Data
6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues

title: 6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues
text:
6.12 C12

Use the subset of 401KSUBS with (fsize = 1); this restricts the analysis to single-person households; see also Computer Exercise C8 in Chapter 4.



The youngest age in the sample is 25. How many people are 25 years old?


Count the number of observations with (age = 25) in the restricted sample ((fsize = 1)). Report the count.


In the model [ netfta = _0 + _1 inc + _2 age + _3 age^2 + u, ] what is the literal interpretation of (_1)? By itself, is it of much interest?


(_1) is the partial effect of income on net financial assets, holding age fixed: (netfta / inc). By itself, it may be of interest as the marginal propensity to save in financial assets. However, the model is quadratic in age, so the marginal effect of income is constant in this specification.


Estimate the model from part (ii) and report the results in standard form. Are you concerned that the coefficient on (age) is negative? Explain.


Estimate the regression and report coefficients with standard errors. A negative coefficient on (age) (linear term) is not concerning if the quadratic term is positive; the overall effect of age could be U-shaped. The negative linear term simply indicates that at low ages, the slope may be negative, turning positive after the minimum.


Because the youngest people in the sample are 25, it makes sense to think that, for a given level of income, the lowest average amount of net total financial assets is at age 25. Recall that the partial effect of (age) on (netfta) is (_2 + 2_3 age), so the partial effect at age 25 is (_2 + 2_3(25)). If the minimum occurs exactly at age 25, this partial effect should be zero or positive. Test whether the partial effect at age 25 is zero against a one-sided alternative that it is positive. What do you conclude?


The hypothesis is (H_0: _2 + 50_3 = 0) versus (H_1: _2 + 50_3 > 0). To test, reparameterize the model by defining (= _2 + 50_3). Substitute (_2 = - 50_3) into the model: [ netfta = _0 + _1 inc + (- 50_3) age + _3 age^2 + u ] [ netfta = _0 + _1 inc + age + _3 (age^2 - 50 age) + u ] Run this regression; the coefficient on (age) is () and its (t)-statistic tests (H_0: = 0). Use a one-sided test to see if ( > 0). Conclude whether assets are increasing with age at age 25.
crumbs: PART 1: Regression Analysis with Cross-Sectional Data
6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues

title: 6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues
text:
6.13 C12 (continued)

(_2 + 2_3(25) = _2 + 50_3); call this (_2). Find (_2) and obtain the two-sided (p)-value for testing (H_0: _2 = 0). You should conclude that (_2) is small and very statistically insignificant. [Hint: One way to do this is to estimate the model (netfta = _0 + _1 inc + _2 age + _3 (age - 25)^2 + u), where the intercept, (_0) is different from (_0). There are other ways, too.]

Estimate the reparameterized model: [ netfta = _0 + _1 inc + _2 age + _3 (age - 25)^2 + u ] The coefficient on (age) is (_2 = _2 + 50_3). Its (t)-statistic tests (H_0: _2 = 0). The (p)-value is likely large (e.g., > 0.5), indicating (_2) is not statistically different from zero.

Because the evidence against (H_0: _2 = 0) is very weak, set it to zero and estimate the model [ netfta = _0 + _1 inc + _3 (age - 25)^2 + u. ] In terms of goodness-of-fit, does this model fit better than that in part (ii)?

Compare adjusted (R^2) between this restricted model and the model from part (ii) (which included (age) and (age^2)). If (_2) is insignificant, the restricted model should have similar or slightly higher adjusted (R^2) due to the parsimony penalty.

For the estimated equation in part (v), set (inc = 30) (roughly, the average value) and graph the relationship between (netfta) and (age), but only for (age ). Describe what you see.

With (inc = 30): [ = _0 + _1(30) + _3 (age - 25)^2 ] This is a quadratic that increases as age moves away from 25. Since the minimum is at age 25, net financial assets increase as individuals age beyond 25, reflecting lifecycle savings accumulation.

Check to see whether including a quadratic in (inc) is necessary.

Add (inc^2) to the model and test its significance using a (t)-test. If the coefficient on (inc^2) is statistically insignificant and adjusted (R^2) does not improve, the quadratic in income is not necessary.
crumbs: PART 1: Regression Analysis with Cross-Sectional Data
6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues

title: 6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues
text:
6.14 C13

Use the data in MEAP00 for this exercise.



Estimate the model [ math4 = _0 + _1 lexppp + _2 lenroll + _3 lunch + u ] by OLS, and report the results in the usual form. Is each explanatory variable statistically significant at the 5% level?


Estimate the regression. Results take the form: [ = _0 + _1 lexppp + _2 lenroll + _3 lunch ] with standard errors in parentheses. Check (p)-values: (lexppp) (expenditure per pupil) is typically positive and significant; (lenroll) (log enrollment) may be insignificant; (lunch) (percent eligible for free lunch) is negative and highly significant.

Obtain the fitted values from the regression in part (i). What is the range of fitted values? How does it compare with the range of the actual data on (math4)?

Compute () for all observations. The fitted values range will be narrower than the actual (math4) range because regression shrinks toward the mean. Actual (math4) ranges from 0 to 100 (or close), while fitted values are within a tighter interval.

Obtain the residuals from the regression in part (i). What is the building code of the school that has the largest (positive) residual? Provide an interpretation of this residual.

Identify the observation with the largest positive residual (actual (math4) much higher than predicted). This school performs much better than expected given its expenditure, enrollment, and poverty rate. The building code identifies that school.

Add quadratics of all explanatory variables to the equation, and test them for joint significance. Would you leave them in the model?

Add (lexppp^2), (lenroll^2), (lunch^2) and test joint significance using an (F)-test. If the (p)-value is small (e.g., < 0.05), the quadratics jointly matter. Even if significant, consider whether they improve predictive power meaningfully or if the simpler model is preferred for parsimony.

Returning to the model in part (i), divide the dependent variable and each explanatory variable by its sample standard deviation, and rerun the regression. (Include an intercept unless you also first subtract the mean from each variable.) In terms of standard deviation units, which explanatory variable has the largest effect on the math pass rate?

Create standardized variables: (math4_std = math4 / sd(math4)), (lexppp_std = lexppp / sd(lexppp)), etc. Regress (math4_std) on these standardized regressors (with intercept). The coefficients now represent the effect of a one-standard-deviation increase in each explanatory variable on the math pass rate in standard deviation units. The variable with the largest absolute coefficient has the largest effect.
crumbs: PART 1: Regression Analysis with Cross-Sectional Data
6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues

title: 6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues
text:
6.15 C14

Use the data in BENEFITS to answer this question. It is a school-level data set at the K–5 level on average teacher salary and benefits. See Example 4.10 for background.



Regress (lavgsal) on (bs) and report the results in the usual form. Can you reject (H_0: {bs} = 0) against a two-sided alternative? Can you reject (H_0: {bs} = -1) against (H_1: _{bs} > -1)? Report the (p)-values for both tests.


Estimate: [ = 0 + {bs} bs ] - For (H_0: {bs} = 0): use the (t)-statistic from the output; the two-sided (p)-value is reported. - For (H_0: {bs} = -1): compute (t = ({bs} + 1) / se({bs})); the one-sided (p)-value is the probability that (t >) this value (if (_{bs} > -1)).


Define (lbs = (bs)). Find the range of values for (lbs) and find its standard deviation. How do these compare to the range and standard deviation for (bs)?


Compute summary statistics. (bs) (benefits as a percent of salary) likely has a narrow range and small standard deviation. Taking logs compresses the scale further; the range and standard deviation of (lbs) will be smaller in magnitude but may show more relative variation.


Regress (lavgsal) on (lbs). Does this fit better than the regression from part (i)?


Compare (R^2) (or adjusted (R^2)) between the two models. The log specification may fit better if the relationship is nonlinear.


Estimate the equation [ lavgsal = _0 + _1 bs + _2 lenroll + _3 lstaff + _4 lunch + u ] and report the results in the usual form. What happens to the coefficient on (bs)? Is it now statistically different from zero?


Include controls. The coefficient on (bs) may change magnitude and significance. With additional controls, its (t)-statistic may become smaller or larger depending on omitted variable bias in part (i). Report the new coefficient and its (p)-value.

Interpret the coefficient on (lstaff). Why do you think it is negative?

(lstaff = (staff)) (likely number of staff). A negative coefficient suggests that larger schools (more staff) have lower average salaries, possibly due to economies of scale or because larger schools are in less affluent areas.

Add (lunch^2) to the equation from part (iv). Is it statistically significant? Compute the turning point (minimum value) in the quadratic, and show that it is within the range of the observed data on (lunch). How many values of (lunch) are higher than the calculated turning point?

Add (lunch^2) and test its significance. The turning point (where the effect of lunch on lavgsal is minimized) is (lunch^* = -{lunch} / (2{lunch^2})) (using linear and quadratic coefficients from the model with both terms). Verify that (lunch^) lies within the observed range of (lunch). Count how many observations have (lunch > lunch^).

Based on the findings from part (vi), describe how teacher salaries relate to school poverty rates. In terms of teacher salary, and holding other factors fixed, is it better to teach at a school with (lunch = 0) (no poverty), (lunch = 50), or (lunch = 100) (all kids eligible for the free lunch program)?

Using the estimated quadratic relationship, compute predicted (lavgsal) at (lunch = 0, 50, 100) (holding other variables fixed at their means). The relationship is likely U-shaped: salaries may be higher at very low poverty (due to resources) and at very high poverty (due to compensating differentials), with a minimum somewhere in the middle. Report which level yields the highest predicted salary.
crumbs: PART 1: Regression Analysis with Cross-Sectional Data
6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues

title: 6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues
text:
6.16 C15

Use the data in NCAA_RPI to answer the following questions. These are data on the 2015–2016 men’s NCAA basketball season. The variable winperc is the winning percentage of a team. The variable postrpi is the team’s ranking based on the “rating percentage index” (RPI) computed from actual performance the previous year, 2014–2015. The RPI is a formula based on a team’s winning percentage, its opponents’ winning percentage, and its opponents’ opponents’ winning percentage. The rankings run from one to 350 in the data set of 336 basketball teams.


The variable prerpi is a ranking created by basketball pundits just before the season began, in 2015. The idea is to see if the experts’ evaluations, based on recruiting, transfers, and overall impressions (in addition to the previous season’s performance) adds anything to the rankings from the previous year.



Find the ranges and averages of the variables winperc and postrpi.


Compute summary statistics. winperc is a percentage ranging from 0 to 100 (or 0 to 1 depending on coding). postrpi is a ranking from 1 to approximately 350. Report the min, max, and mean for each.


Run a simple regression of winperc on postrpi. Interpret the coefficient on postrpi. Is the estimate statistically significant at the 1% level? Would you say postrpi explains much of the variation in winperc?


Estimate: [ = _0 + _1 postrpi ] (_1) is expected to be negative (better ranking—lower number—predicts higher winning percentage). Test significance using the (t)-statistic; at the 1% level, (p < 0.01) indicates significance. The (R^2) will likely be modest (e.g., 0.10–0.30), indicating postrpi explains some but not most of the variation.


In part (ii), replace postrpi with ((postrpi)). Which functional form, postrpi or ((postrpi)), explains more of the variation in winperc? What is the change in the predicted winning percentage if postrpi increases by 10%?


Compare (R^2) between the two models. For the log model: [ = _0 + _1 (postrpi) ] A 10% increase in postrpi means ((postrpi) ). The predicted change in winperc is approximately (_1 ).


To the regression in part (iii), add ((prerpi)). Does this pre-season RPI rank help predict winperc once ((postrpi)) is included?


Estimate: [ = _0 + _1 (postrpi) + _2 (prerpi) ] Test (H_0: _2 = 0). If (_2) is statistically significant, the pre-season ranking adds predictive power beyond the previous year’s actual performance.


Instead of using the logarithms of the RPI variables, estimate the equation [ winperc = _0 + _1 postrpi + _2 postrpi^2 + _3 prerpi + u ] Explain what it means for (_1) to be negative and (_2) positive. Are they each statistically significant?


A negative (_1) and positive (_2) imply a U-shaped relationship: winning percentage decreases as ranking worsens (postrpi increases) initially, but eventually the decline slows and may reverse. This could reflect that very poorly ranked teams have little variation in winning percentage. Test significance using (t)-tests.


Using the adjusted (R)-squared, does equation (v) fit better or does (iv)?


Compare adjusted (R^2) between the log model (iv) and the quadratic model (v). The higher adjusted (R^2) indicates better fit.


In part (v), now does prerpi have extra predictive power for winperc?


Test (H_0: _3 = 0) in the quadratic model. If the (t)-statistic is significant, prerpi helps predict winperc even after controlling for a quadratic in postrpi.


Does the estimated equation from part (v) predict any negative values? What about any values above 100?


Compute fitted values () and check the range. If the quadratic extrapolates poorly at extreme postrpi values, some predictions may fall outside the [0,100] range.


Compute the turning point in the quadratic estimated in part (v) and use it to sketch the relationship between winperc and postrpi (holding prerpi fixed). Does it make sense? (Hint: What is the range of values for postrpi?)


The turning point for the quadratic in postrpi is (postrpi^* = -_1 / (2_2)). If (_2 > 0), this is a minimum. If (postrpi^*) lies outside the observed range of postrpi (1 to ~350), the relationship is monotonic over the sample. Sketch the curve: winning percentage declines as ranking worsens until the turning point, then increases—but if the turning point is beyond the sample range, the relationship is simply decreasing.
crumbs: PART 1: Regression Analysis with Cross-Sectional Data
6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues

title: 6  Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues
text:
6.17 C16

Use the data JTRAIN98 to answer the following questions.



What are the units of measurement of the labor market earnings variable earn98? Find the mean and standard deviation of earn98 in the sample. For what percentage of the sample is earn98 equal to zero?


earn98 is likely in dollars (or perhaps hundreds/thousands). Compute mean, standard deviation, and the fraction of observations with earn98 = 0 (non-workers).


Define two new variables, earn98dol = 1,000 · earn98 and earn98cents = 1,000 · earn98dol. How do the averages and standard deviations of these two variables compare with the corresponding quantities in part (i)? Do your findings make sense?


Scaling by constants multiplies the mean and standard deviation by the same factor. If earn98 is in thousands of dollars, multiplying by 1,000 gives dollars, and multiplying again by 1,000 gives cents. The relationships are linear scaling.


Use OLS to estimate a model with two explanatory variables, educ and age. Use each of earn98, earn98dol, and earn98cents as the dependent variable. How do the coefficients compare across the three regressions? Do your findings make sense?


If the dependent variable is multiplied by a constant (c), the coefficients are multiplied by (c) as well. So ({educ}) in the earn98dol regression will be 1,000 times that in the earn98 regression, and ({educ}) in the earn98cents regression will be (1,000 ,000 = 1,000,000) times that in the earn98 regression. Standard errors scale similarly, so (t)-statistics and (R^2) remain unchanged.


From Problem 12(v), the percentage change in average earn98 when (educ = 1) (holding age fixed) can be estimated as (_{educ} / ). What is this estimate from part (iii)? Does it matter how the 1998 earnings are measured?


This approximation holds for the level model when earnings are positive. Since scaling the dependent variable scales both the numerator and denominator proportionally (if () also scales), the ratio (_{educ} / ) is invariant to units of measurement. Thus the estimated percentage effect is the same regardless of whether earnings are measured in dollars, thousands, or cents.


Now define learn98pl = log(1 + earn98), where “pl” is short for “plus one.” Using the same transformation, generate learn98dolpl and learn98centspl. Is there any obvious relationship between the averages of learn98pl and learn98dolpl?


Because ((1 + y)) is not linear in (y), scaling (y) by a constant does not simply scale the transformed variable. If earnings are scaled by a factor (c), ((1 + c y)) is not equal to a linear transformation of ((1 + y)). Therefore, averages will differ in a non-proportional way.


Now use learn98pl, learn98dolpl, and learn98centspl separately as dependent variables where educ and age are the explanatory variables. Compare the three coefficients on educ and comment on the pattern. What does this say about the importance of units of measurement when using ((1 + y)) as a transformation?


Since ((1 + y)) is nonlinear, changing the units of (y) alters the transformation’s shape. The coefficients on educ will differ across regressions not by a constant factor. This illustrates that ((1 + y)) is not scale-invariant; the choice of units affects the estimated coefficients. For large (y), ((1 + y) (y)), which is scale-invariant up to an additive constant, but for small (y) (including zeros), the additive 1 matters and makes the transformation sensitive to scaling.

title: 7  Multiple Regression Analysis with Qualitative Information
text:
7.2 C2 [WAGE2]

Use the data in WAGE2 for this exercise. (i) Estimate the model
\[\log(wage) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 educ + \beta_2 exper + \beta_3 tenure + \beta_4 married + \beta_5 black + \beta_6 south + \beta_7 urban + u\]
and report the results in the usual form. Holding other factors fixed, what is the approximate difference in monthly salary between blacks and nonblacks? Is this difference statistically significant? (ii) Add the variables \(exper^2\) and \(tenure^2\) to the equation and show that they are jointly insignificant at even the 20% level. (iii) Extend the original model to allow the return to education to depend on race and test whether the return to education does depend on race. (iv) Again, start with the original model, but now allow wages to differ across four groups of people: married and black, married and nonblack, single and black, and single and nonblack. What is the estimated wage differential between married blacks and married nonblacks?
crumbs: PART 1: Regression Analysis with Cross-Sectional Data
7  Multiple Regression Analysis with Qualitative Information

title: 7  Multiple Regression Analysis with Qualitative Information
text:
7.3 C3 [MLB1]

A model that allows major league baseball player salary to differ by position is
\[\log(salary) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 years + \beta_2 gamesyr + \beta_3 bavg + \beta_4 hrunsy + \beta_5 rbisyr + \beta_6 runsyr + \beta_7 fldperc + \beta_8 allstar + \beta_9 frstbase + \beta_{10} scndbase + \beta_{11} thrdbase + \beta_{12} shrtstop + \beta_{13} catcher + u,\]
where outfield is the base group.



State the null hypothesis that, controlling for other factors, catchers and outfielders earn, on average, the same amount. Test this hypothesis using the data in MLB1 and comment on the size of the estimated salary differential.
State and test the null hypothesis that there is no difference in average salary across positions, once other factors have been controlled for.
Are the results from parts (i) and (ii) consistent? If not, explain what is happening.

title: 7  Multiple Regression Analysis with Qualitative Information
text:
7.4 C4 [GPA2]

Use the data in GPA2 for this exercise. (i) Consider the equation
\[colgpa = \beta_0 + \beta_1 hsize + \beta_2 hsize^2 + \beta_3 hsperc + \beta_4 sat + \beta_5 female + \beta_6 athlete + u,\]
where colgpa is cumulative college grade point average; hsize is size of high school graduating class, in hundreds; hsperc is academic percentile in graduating class; sat is combined SAT score; female is a binary gender variable; and athlete is a binary variable, which is equal to one for student athletes. What are your expectations for the coefficients in this equation? Which ones are you unsure about? (ii) Estimate the equation in part (i) and report the results in the usual form. What is the estimated GPA differential between athletes and nonathletes? Is it statistically significant? (iii) Drop sat from the model and reestimate the equation. Now, what is the estimated effect of being an athlete? Discuss why the estimate is different than that obtained in part (ii). (iv) In the model from part (i), allow the effect of being an athlete to differ by gender and test the null hypothesis that there is no ceteris paribus difference between women athletes and women nonathletes. (v) Does the effect of sat on colgpa differ by gender? Justify your answer.

title: 7  Multiple Regression Analysis with Qualitative Information
text:
7.5 C5 [CEOSAL1]

In Problem 2 in Chapter 4, we added the return on the firm’s stock, ros, to a model explaining CEO salary; ros turned out to be insignificant. Now, define a dummy variable, rosneg, which is equal to one if \(ros < 0\) and equal to zero if \(ros \geq 0\). Use CEOSAL1 to estimate the model
\[\log(salary) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \log(sales) + \beta_2 roe + \beta_3 rosneg + u.\]
Discuss the interpretation and statistical significance of \(\hat{\beta}_3\).

title: 7  Multiple Regression Analysis with Qualitative Information
text:
7.6 C6 [SLEEP75]

Use the data in SLEEP75 for this exercise. The equation of interest is
\[sleep = \beta_0 + \beta_1 totwrk + \beta_2 educ + \beta_3 age + \beta_4 age^2 + \beta_5 yngkid + u.\]

Estimate this equation separately for men and women and report the results in the usual form. Are there notable differences in the two estimated equations?
Compute the Chow test for equality of the parameters in the sleep equation for men and women. Use the form of the test that adds male and the interaction terms \(male \cdot totwrk, \dots, male \cdot yngkid\) and uses the full set of observations. What are the relevant \(df\) for the test? Should you reject the null at the 5% level?
Now, allow for a different intercept for males and females and determine whether the interaction terms involving male are jointly significant.
Given the results from parts (ii) and (iii), what would be your final model?

7.7 C7 [WAGE1]

Use the data in WAGE1 for this exercise. (i) Use equation (7.18) to estimate the gender differential when \(educ = 12.5\), which is very close to the average education in the sample. Compare this with the estimated differential when \(educ = 0\).



Run the regression used to obtain (7.18), but with \(female \cdot (educ - 12.5)\) replacing \(female \cdot educ\). How do you interpret the coefficient on female now?
Is the coefficient on female in part (ii) statistically significant? Compare this with (7.18) and comment.

title: 7  Multiple Regression Analysis with Qualitative Information
text:
7.8 C8 [LOANAPP]

Use the data in LOANAPP for this exercise. The binary variable to be explained is approve, which is equal to one if a mortgage loan to an individual was approved. The key explanatory variable is white, a dummy variable equal to one if the applicant was white. The other applicants in the data set are black and Hispanic. To test for discrimination in the mortgage loan market, a linear probability model can be used:
\[approve = \beta_0 + \beta_1 white + \text{other factors}.\]

If there is discrimination against minorities, and the appropriate factors have been controlled for, what is the sign of \(\beta_1\)?
Regress approve on white and report the results in the usual form. Interpret the coefficient on white. Is it statistically significant? Is it practically large?
As controls, add the variables hrat, obrat, loanprc, unem, male, married, dep, sch, cosign, chist, pubrec, mortlat1, mortlat2, and vr. What happens to the coefficient on white? Is there still evidence of discrimination against nonwhites?
Now, allow the effect of race to interact with the variable measuring other obligations as a percentage of income (obrat). Is the interaction term significant?
Using the model from part (iv), what is the effect of being white on the probability of approval when \(obrat = 32\), which is roughly the mean value in the sample? Obtain a 95% confidence interval for this effect.
crumbs: PART 1: Regression Analysis with Cross-Sectional Data

title: 7  Multiple Regression Analysis with Qualitative Information
text:
7.9 C9 [401KSUBS]

There has been much interest in whether the presence of 401(k) pension plans, available to many U.S. workers, increases net savings. The data set 401KSUBS contains information on net financial assets (nettfa), family income (inc), a binary variable for eligibility in a 401(k) plan (e401k), and several other variables. (i) What fraction of the families in the sample are eligible for participation in a 401(k) plan? (ii) Estimate a linear probability model explaining 401(k) eligibility in terms of income, age, and gender. Include income and age in quadratic form, and report the results in the usual form. (iii) Would you say that 401(k) eligibility is independent of income and age? What about gender? Explain. (iv) Obtain the fitted values from the linear probability model estimated in part (ii). Are any fitted values negative or greater than one? (v) Using the fitted values \(\hat{e401k}_i\) from part (iv), define \(\tilde{e401k}_i = 1\) if \(\hat{e401k}_i \geq 0.5\) and \(\tilde{e401k}_i = 0\) if \(\hat{e401k}_i < 0.5\). Out of 9,275 families, how many are predicted to be eligible for a 401(k) plan? (vi) For the 5,638 families not eligible for a 401(k) plan, what percentage of these are predicted not to be eligible, using the predictor \(\tilde{e401k}_i\)? For the 3,637 families eligible for a 401(k) plan, what percentage are predicted to be eligible? (It is helpful if your econometrics package has a “tabulate” command.) (vii) The overall percent correctly predicted is about 64.9%. Do you think this is a complete description of how well the model does, given your answers in part (vi)? (viii) Add the variable pira as an explanatory variable to the linear probability model. Other things equal, if a family has someone with an individual retirement account, how much higher is the estimated probability that the family is eligible for a 401(k) plan? Is it statistically different from zero at the 10% level?

I have transcribed the content from the provided images, which feature econometrics computer exercises.

title: 7  Multiple Regression Analysis with Qualitative Information
text:
7.10 C10 [NBASAL]
Use the data in NBASAL for this exercise. * (i) Estimate a linear regression model relating points per game to experience in the league and position (guard, forward, or center). Include experience in quadratic form and use centers as the base group. Report the results in the usual form. * (ii) Why do you not include all three position dummy variables in part (i)? * (iii) Holding experience fixed, does a guard score more than a center? How much more? Is the difference statistically significant? * (iv) Now, add marital status to the equation. Holding position and experience fixed, are married players more productive (based on points per game)? * (v) Add interactions of marital status with both experience variables. In this expanded model, is there strong evidence that marital status affects points per game? * (vi) Estimate the model from part (iv) but use assists per game as the dependent variable. Are there any notable differences from part (iv)? Discuss.
crumbs: PART 1: Regression Analysis with Cross-Sectional Data
7  Multiple Regression Analysis with Qualitative Information

title: 7  Multiple Regression Analysis with Qualitative Information
text:
7.11 C11 [401KSUBS]
Use the data in 401KSUBS for this exercise. * (i) Compute the average, standard deviation, minimum, and maximum values of \(netfa\) in the sample. * (ii) Test the hypothesis that average \(netfa\) does not differ by 401(k) eligibility status; use a two-sided alternative. What is the dollar amount of the estimated difference? * (iii) From part (ii) of Computer Exercise C9, it is clear that \(e401k\) is not exogenous in a simple regression model; at a minimum, it changes by income and age. Estimate a multiple linear regression model for \(netfa\) that includes income, age, and \(e401k\) as explanatory variables. The income and age variables should appear as quadratics. Now, what is the estimated dollar effect of 401(k) eligibility? * (iv) To the model estimated in part (iii), add the interactions \(e401k \cdot (age - 41)\) and \(e401k \cdot (age - 41)^2\). Note that the average age in the sample is about 41, so that in the new model, the coefficient on \(e401k\) is the estimated effect of 401(k) eligibility at the average age. Which interaction term is significant? * (v) Comparing the estimates from parts (iii) and (iv), do the estimated effects of 401(k) eligibility at age 41 differ much? Explain. * (vi) Now, drop the interaction terms from the model, but define five family size dummy variables: \(fsize1\), \(fsize2\), \(fsize3\), \(fsize4\), and \(fsize5\). The variable \(fsize5\) is unity for families with five or more members. Include the family size dummies in the model estimated from part (iii); be sure to choose a base group. Are the family dummies significant at the 1% level? * (vii) Now, do a Chow test for the model \[nettfa = \beta_0 + \beta_1 inc + \beta_2 inc^2 + \beta_3 age + \beta_4 age^2 + \beta_5 e401k + u\] across the five family size categories, allowing for intercept differences. The restricted sum of squared residuals, \(SSR_r\), is obtained from part (vi) because that regression assumes all slopes are the same. The unrestricted sum of squared residuals is \(SSR_{ur} = SSR_1 + SSR_2 + \dots + SSR_5\), where \(SSR_f\) is the sum of squared residuals for the equation estimated using only family size \(f\). You should convince yourself that there are 30 parameters in the unrestricted model (5 intercepts plus 25 slopes) and 10 parameters in the restricted model (5 intercepts plus 5 slopes). Therefore, the number of restrictions being tested is \(q = 20\), and the \(df\) for the unrestricted model is \(9,275 - 30 = 9,245\).

title: 7  Multiple Regression Analysis with Qualitative Information
text:
7.12 C12 [BEAUTY]
Use the data set in BEAUTY, which contains a subset of the variables (but more usable observations than in the regressions) reported by Hamermesh and Biddle (1994). * (i) Find the separate fractions of men and women that are classified as having above average looks. Are more people rated as having above average or below average looks? * (ii) Test the null hypothesis that the population fractions of above-average-looking women and men are the same. Report the one-sided \(p\)-value that the fraction is higher for women. (Hint: Estimating a simple linear probability model is easiest.) * (iii) Estimate the model \(\log(wage) = \beta_0 + \beta_1belavg + \beta_2abvavg + u\) separately for men and women and report the results in the usual form. Interpret the coefficient on \(belavg\) in each case. State the hypothesis \(H_0: \beta_1 = 0\) against \(H_1: \beta_1 < 0\) in words, and find the \(p\)-values for both men and women. * (iv) Is there convincing evidence that women with above-average looks earn more than women with average looks? Explain. * (v) Add explanatory variables including \(educ\), \(exper\), \(exper^2\), \(union\), \(goodhlth\), \(black\), \(married\), \(south\), \(bigcity\), \(smllcity\), and \(service\) for both men and women. Do the effects of the “looks” variables change in important ways? * (vi) Use the \(SSR\) form of the Chow \(F\) statistic to test whether the slopes of the regression functions in part (v) differ between men and women, allowing for an intercept shift under the null.


7.12.1 C13 [APPLE]
Use the data in APPLE to answer this question. * (i) Define a binary variable \(ecobuy = 1\) if \(ecolbs > 0\) and \(ecobuy = 0\) if \(ecolbs = 0\) (indicating if a family would buy any ecologically friendly apples at the given prices). What fraction of families claim they would buy ecolabeled apples? * (ii) Estimate the linear probability model (LPM): \(ecobuy = \beta_0 + \beta_1ecoprc + \beta_2regprc + \beta_3faminc + \beta_4hhsize + \beta_5educ + \beta_6age + u\). Report the results and carefully interpret the coefficients on the price variables. * (iii) Test if the nonprice variables are jointly significant in the LPM using the usual \(F\) statistic. Which nonprice variable seems to have the most important effect on the decision to buy ecolabeled apples? * (iv) Replace \(faminc\) with \(\log(faminc)\) in the previous model. Which model fits the data better? Interpret the coefficient on \(\log(faminc)\). * (v) In the \(\log(faminc)\) model, how many estimated probabilities are negative or greater than one? Should you be concerned? * (vi) Compute the percent correctly predicted for each outcome (\(ecobuy = 0\) and \(ecobuy = 1\)) for the model in part (iv). Which outcome is best predicted?



7.12.2 C14 [CHARITY]
Use the data in CHARITY to answer this question. * (i) Estimate an LPM relating \(respond\) (dummy for responding with a contribution) to \(resplast\) (dummy for responding to the previous mailing) and \(avggift\) (average of past gifts). Interpret the coefficient on \(resplast\). * (ii) Does the average value of past gifts affect the probability of responding? * (iii) Add the variable \(propresp\) (proportion of times the person responded to past mailings) to the model. Interpret its coefficient, noting that an increase of one is the largest possible change. * (iv) What happened to the coefficient on \(resplast\) when \(propresp\) was added? Does this make sense? * (v) Add \(mailyear\) (number of mailings per year) to the model. How big is its estimated effect? Why might this not be a good estimate of the causal effect of mailings on responding?
