(v) To the LPM in part (ii) add the variables age, agesq, educ, educsq, married, famsize, white, northeast, midwest, south, centcity, outercity, qrt1, qrt2, and qrt3. What happens to the coefficient on abuse and its statistical significance? (vi) Estimate a probit model using the variables in part (v). Find the APE of abuse and its \(t\) statistic. Is the estimated effect now identical to that for the linear model? Is it “close”? (vii) Variables indicating the overall health of each man are also included in the data set. Is it obvious that such variables should be included as controls? Explain. (viii) Why might abuse be properly thought of as endogenous in the employ equation? Do you think the variables mothalc and fathalc, indicating whether a man’s mother or father were alcoholics, are sensible instrumental variables for abuse? (ix) Estimate the LPM underlying part (v) by 2SLS, where mothalc and fathalc act as IVs for abuse. Is the difference between the 2SLS and OLS coefficients practically large? (x) Use the test described in Section 15-5 to test whether abuse is endogenous in the LPM.
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
17  Limited Dependent Variable Models and Sample Selection Corrections

title: 17  Limited Dependent Variable Models and Sample Selection Corrections
text:
17.16 C16 [CRIME1]

Use the data in CRIME1 to answer this question. (i) For the OLS estimates reported in Table 17.5, find the heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors. In terms of statistical significance of the coefficients, are there any notable changes? (ii) Obtain the fully robust standard errors—that is, those that do not even require assumption (17.35)—for the Poisson regression estimates in the second column. (This requires that you have a statistical package that computes the fully robust standard errors.) Compare the fully robust 95% confidence interval for \(\beta_{pcnv}\) with that obtained using the standard error in Table 17.5.
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
17  Limited Dependent Variable Models and Sample Selection Corrections

title: 18  Advanced Time Series Topics
text: 18.1 C1 [WAGEPRC]
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
18  Advanced Time Series Topics

title: 18  Advanced Time Series Topics
text: Use the data in WAGEPRC for this exercise. Problem 5 in Chapter 11 gave estimates of a finite distributed lag model of gprice on gwage, where 12 lags of gwage are used. (i) Estimate a simple geometric DL model of gprice on gwage. In particular, estimate equation (18.11) by OLS. What are the estimated impact propensity and LRP? Sketch the estimated lag distribution. (ii) Compare the estimated IP and LRP to those obtained in Problem 5 in Chapter 11. How do the estimated lag distributions compare? (iii) Now, estimate the rational distributed lag model from (18.16). Sketch the lag distribution and compare the estimated IP and LRP to those obtained in part (ii).
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
18  Advanced Time Series Topics

title: 18  Advanced Time Series Topics
text:
18.2 C2 [HSEINV]

Use the data in HSEINV for this exercise. (i) Test for a unit root in \(\log(invpc)\), including a linear time trend and two lags of \(\Delta\log(invpc_t)\). Use a 5% significance level. (ii) Use the approach from part (i) to test for a unit root in \(\log(price)\). (iii) Given the outcomes in parts (i) and (ii), does it make sense to test for cointegration between \(\log(invpc)\) and \(\log(price)\)?
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
18  Advanced Time Series Topics

title: 18  Advanced Time Series Topics
text:
18.3 C3 [VOLAT]

Use the data in VOLAT for this exercise. (i) Estimate an AR(3) model for pcip. Now, add a fourth lag and verify that it is very insignificant. (ii) To the AR(3) model from part (i), add three lags of pcsp to test whether pcsp Granger causes pcip. Carefully, state your conclusion. (iii) To the model in part (ii), add three lags of the change in i3, the three-month T-bill rate. Does pcsp Granger cause pcip conditional on past \(\Delta i3\)?
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
18  Advanced Time Series Topics

title: 18  Advanced Time Series Topics
text:
18.4 C4

In testing for cointegration between gfr and pe in Example 18.5, add \(t^2\) to equation (18.32) to obtain the OLS residuals. Include one lag in the augmented DF test. The 5% critical value for the test is \(-4.15\).
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
18  Advanced Time Series Topics

title: 18  Advanced Time Series Topics
text:
18.5 C5 [INTQRT]

Use INTQRT for this exercise. (i) In Example 18.7, we estimated an error correction model for the holding yield on six-month T-bills, where one lag of the holding yield on three-month T-bills is the explanatory variable. We assumed that the cointegration parameter was one in the equation \(hy6_t = \alpha + \beta hy3_{t-1} + u_t\). Now, add the lead change, \(\Delta hy3_t\), the contemporaneous change, \(\Delta hy3_{t-1}\), and the lagged change, \(\Delta hy3_{t-2}\), of \(\Delta hy3_{t-1}\). That is, estimate the equation \[hy6_t = \alpha + \beta hy3_{t-1} + \phi_0 \Delta hy3_t + \phi_1 \Delta hy3_{t-1} + \rho_1 \Delta hy3_{t-2} + e_t\] and report the results in equation form. Test H\(_0\): \(\beta = 1\) against a two-sided alternative. Assume that the lead and lag are sufficient so that \(\{hy3_{t-1}\}\) is strictly exogenous in this equation and do not worry about serial correlation. (ii) To the error correction model in (18.39), add \(\Delta hy3_{t-2}\) and \((hy6_{t-2} - hy3_{t-3})\). Are these terms jointly significant? What do you conclude about the appropriate error correction model?
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
18  Advanced Time Series Topics

title: 18  Advanced Time Series Topics
text:
18.6 C6 [PHILLIPS]

Use the data in PHILLIPS for this exercise. (i) Estimate the models represented in equations (18.48) and (18.49) using the data through 2015. Do the parameter estimates change much compared with (18.48) and (18.49)? (ii) Use the new equations to forecast \(unem_{2016}\); round to two decimal places. Which equation produces a better forecast? (iii) Use the equation that includes \(inf_{t-1}\), estimated in part (i), to forecast \(unem_{2017}\). You will need to obtain the 2016 values for unem and inf. Next, reestimate the parameters using the data through 2016, and use the updated estimates to forecast \(unem_{2017}\). Does using the extra year of data to obtain the parameter estimates produce a better forecast? (iv) Use the equation that includes \(inf_{t-1}\), estimated in part (i), to obtain a two-step-ahead forecast of unem. You will need the forecast of \(unem_{2016}\) from part (ii). Also, use an AR(1) model for inflation, using the data through 2015, to forecast 2016 inflation.
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
18  Advanced Time Series Topics

title: 18  Advanced Time Series Topics
text:
18.7 C7 [BARIUM]

Use the data in BARIUM for this exercise. (i) Estimate the linear trend model \(chnimp_t = \alpha + \beta t + u_t\), using the first 119 observations (this excludes the last 12 months of observations for 1988). What is the standard error of the regression? (ii) Now, estimate an AR(1) model for chnimp, again using all data but the last 12 months. Compare the standard error of the regression with that from part (i). Which model provides a better in-sample fit? (iii) Use the models from parts (i) and (ii) to compute the one-step-ahead forecast errors for the 12 months in 1988. (You should obtain 12 forecast errors for each method.) Compute and compare the RMSEs and the MAEs for the two methods. Which forecasting method works better out-of-sample for one-step-ahead forecasts? (iv) Add monthly dummy variables to the regression from part (i). Are these jointly significant? (Do not worry about the slight serial correlation in the errors from this regression when doing the joint test.)
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
18  Advanced Time Series Topics

title: 18  Advanced Time Series Topics
text:
18.8 C8 [FERTIL3]

Use the data in FERTIL3 for this exercise. (i) Graph gfr against time. Does it contain a clear upward or downward trend over the entire sample period? (ii) Using the data through 1979, estimate a cubic time trend model for gfr (that is, regress gfr on \(t\), \(t^2\), and \(t^3\), along with an intercept). Comment on the \(R\)-squared of the regression. (iii) Using the model in part (ii), compute the mean absolute error of the one-step-ahead forecast errors for the years 1980 through 1984. (iv) Using the data through 1979, regress \(\Delta gfr_t\) on a constant only. Is the constant statistically different from zero? Does it make sense to assume that any drift term is zero, if we assume that \(gfr_t\) follows a random walk?



Now, forecast \(gfr\) for 1980 through 1984, using a random walk model: the forecast of \(gfr_{n+1}\) is simply \(gfr_n\). Find the MAE. How does it compare with the MAE from part (iii)? Which method of forecasting do you prefer?


Now, estimate an AR(2) model for \(gfr\), again using the data only through 1979. Is the second lag significant?
Obtain the MAE for 1980 through 1984, using the AR(2) model. Does this more general model work better out-of-sample than the random walk model?
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
18  Advanced Time Series Topics

title: 18  Advanced Time Series Topics
text:
18.9 C9 [CONSUMP]

Use CONSUMP for this exercise. (i) Let \(y_t\) be real per capita disposable income. Use the data through 1989 to estimate the model \[y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \rho y_{t-1} + u_t\] and report the results in the usual form. (ii) Use the estimated equation from part (i) to forecast \(y\) in 1990. What is the forecast error? (iii) Compute the mean absolute error of the one-step-ahead forecasts for the 1990s, using the parameters estimated in part (i). (iv) Now, compute the MAE over the same period, but drop \(y_{t-1}\) from the equation. Is it better to include \(y_{t-1}\) in the model or not?
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
18  Advanced Time Series Topics

title: 18  Advanced Time Series Topics
text:
18.10 C10 [INTQRT]

Use the data in INTQRT for this exercise. (i) Using the data from all but the last four years (16 quarters), estimate an AR(1) model for \(\Delta r6_t\). (We use the difference because it appears that \(r6_t\) has a unit root.) Find the RMSE of the one-step-ahead forecasts for \(\Delta r6\), using the last 16 quarters. (ii) Now, add the error correction term \(spr_{t-1} = r6_{t-1} - r3_{t-1}\) to the equation from part (i). (This assumes that the cointegrating parameter is one.) Compute the RMSE for the last 16 quarters. Does the error correction term help with out-of-sample forecasting in this case? (iii) Now, estimate the cointegrating parameter, rather than setting it to one. Use the last 16 quarters again to produce the out-of-sample RMSE. How does this compare with the forecasts from parts (i) and (ii)? (iv) Would your conclusions change if you wanted to predict \(r6\) rather than \(\Delta r6\)? Explain.
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
18  Advanced Time Series Topics

title: 18  Advanced Time Series Topics
text:
18.11 C11 [VOLAT]

Use the data in VOLAT for this exercise. (i) Confirm that \(lsp500 = \log(sp500)\) and \(lip = \log(ip)\) appear to contain unit roots. Use Dickey-Fuller tests with four lagged changes and do the tests with and without a linear time trend. (ii) Run a simple regression of \(lsp500\) on \(lip\). Comment on the sizes of the \(t\) statistic and \(R\)-squared. (iii) Use the residuals from part (ii) to test whether \(lsp500\) and \(lip\) are cointegrated. Use the standard Dickey-Fuller test and the ADF test with two lags. What do you conclude? (iv) Add a linear time trend to the regression from part (ii) and now test for cointegration using the same tests from part (iii). (v) Does it appear that stock prices and real economic activity have a long-run equilibrium relationship?
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
18  Advanced Time Series Topics

title: 18  Advanced Time Series Topics
text:
18.12 C12 [VOLAT]

This exercise also uses the data from VOLAT. Computer Exercise C11 studies the long-run relationship between stock prices and industrial production. Here, you will study the question of Granger causality using the percentage changes. (i) Estimate an AR(3) model for \(pcip_t\), the percentage change in industrial production (reported at an annualized rate). Show that the second and third lags are jointly significant at the 2.5% level. (ii) Add one lag of \(pcsp_t\) to the equation estimated in part (i). Is the lag statistically significant? What does this tell you about Granger causality between the growth in industrial production and the growth in stock prices?



Redo part (ii) but obtain a heteroskedasticity-robust \(t\) statistic. Does the robust test change your conclusions from part (ii)?
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
18  Advanced Time Series Topics

title: 18  Advanced Time Series Topics
text:
18.13 C13 [TRAFFIC2]

Use the data in TRAFFIC2 for this exercise. These monthly data, on traffic accidents in California over the years 1981 to 1989, were used in Computer Exercise C11 in Chapter 10. (i) Using the standard Dickey-Fuller regression, test whether \(ltotacc_t\) has a unit root. Can you reject a unit root at the 2.5% level? (ii) Now, add two lagged changes to the test from part (i) and compute the augmented Dickey-Fuller test. What do you conclude? (iii) Add a linear time trend to the ADF regression from part (ii). Now what happens? (iv) Given the findings from parts (i) through (iii), what would you say is the best characterization of \(ltotacc_t\): an I(1) process or an I(0) process about a linear time trend? (v) Test the percentage of fatalities, \(prcfat_t\), for a unit root, using two lags in an ADF regression. In this case, does it matter whether you include a linear time trend?
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
18  Advanced Time Series Topics

title: 18  Advanced Time Series Topics
text:
18.14 C14 [MINWAGE.DTA]

Use the data in MINWAGE.DTA for sector 232 to answer the following questions. (i) Confirm that \(lwage232_t\) and \(lemp232_t\) are best characterized as I(1) processes. Use the augmented DF test with one lag of \(gwage232\) and \(gemp232\), respectively, and a linear time trend. Is there any doubt that these series should be assumed to have unit roots? (ii) Regress \(lemp232_t\) on \(lwage232_t\) and test for cointegration, both with and without a time trend, allowing for two lags in the augmented Engle-Granger test. What do you conclude? (iii) Now regress \(lemp232_t\) on log of the real wage rate, \(lrwage232_t = lwage232_t - lcpi_t\), and a time trend. Do you find cointegration? Are they “closer” to being cointegrated when you use real wages rather than nominal wages? (iv) What are some factors that might be missing from the cointegrating regression in part (iii)?
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
18  Advanced Time Series Topics

title: 18  Advanced Time Series Topics
text:
18.15 C15 [BEVERIDGE]

This question asks you to study the so-called Beveridge Curve from the perspective of cointegration analysis. The U.S. monthly data from December 2000 through February 2012 are in BEVERIDGE. (i) Test for a unit root in \(urate\) using the usual Dickey-Fuller test (with a constant) and the augmented DF with two lags of \(curate\). What do you conclude? Are the lags of \(curate\) in the augmented DF test statistically significant? Does it matter to the outcome of the unit root test? (ii) Repeat part (i) but with the vacancy rate, \(vrate\). (iii) Assuming that \(urate\) and \(vrate\) are both I(1), the Beveridge Curve, \[urate_t = \alpha + \beta vrate + u_t,\] only makes sense if \(urate\) and \(vrate\) are cointegrated (with cointegrating parameter \(\beta < 0\)). Test for cointegration using the Engle-Granger test with no lags. Are \(urate\) and \(vrate\) cointegrated at the 10% significance level? What about at the 5% level? (iv) Obtain the leads and lags estimator with \(cvrate_t\), \(cvrate_{t-1}\), and \(cvrate_{t+1}\) as the I(0) explanatory variables added to the equation in part (iii). Obtain the Newey-West standard error for \(\hat{\beta}\) using four lags (so \(g = 4\) in the notation of Section 12-5). What is the resulting 95% confidence interval for \(\beta\)? How does it compare with the confidence interval that is not robust to serial correlation (or heteroskedasticity)? (v) Redo the Engle-Granger test but with two lags in the augmented DF regression. What happens? What do you conclude about the robustness of the claim that \(urate\) and \(vrate\) are cointegrated?
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
18  Advanced Time Series Topics

title: 18  Advanced Time Series Topics
text:
18.16 C16 [PHILLIPS]

Use the data in PHILLIPS for this exercise. (i) Using all of the years—through 2017—run the regression \(\Delta inf_t\) on \(inf_{t-1}\) (and an intercept) and test the null hypothesis that \(\{inf_t\}\) is I(1) against the alternative that it is I(0). At what significance level do you reject the null hypothesis?



What is the estimated value of \(\rho\) from part (i)? Would you say it is practically different from one?
Now run the augmented Dickey-Fuller regression by including \(\Delta inf_{t-1}\) as a regressor. Do your conclusions from part (i) change? Does it appear \(\Delta inf_{t-1}\) needs to be in the regression?
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
18  Advanced Time Series Topics

title: 18  Advanced Time Series Topics
text:
18.17 C17

Consider Example 18.11, where you should define the variable \(d\bar{y}_t\) using CA as the treated state and the southern states Alabama (AL), Arkansas (AR), Louisiana (LA), and Mississippi (MS) as the four controls. (i) Using the data up through 1988, regress \(\Delta d\bar{y}_t\) on \(d\bar{y}_{t-1}\). Does it appear that \(d\bar{y}_t\) has a unit root? What is the implied estimate of \(\rho\) in the AR(1) model? (ii) Add a time trend to the test in part (i). Now what do you conclude about whether \(d\bar{y}_t \sim \text{I}(1)\)? (iii) Produce a graph as in Figure 18.3. Does the parallel trends assumption for \(y_{CA,t}\) and \(\bar{y}_{0t}\) appear to be satisfied? (iv) Run the regression of \(d\bar{y}_t\) on \(year_t\) (including an intercept) on the data through 1988. Obtain the residuals for all time periods and produce a graph as in Figure 18.4. Does it appear the trend in \(d\bar{y}_t\) before the intervention has been eliminated? (v) Estimate equation (18.73) and report the coefficient on \(post_t\) along with its Newey-West standard error (lag = 2). How does it compare with the estimates using the western and midwestern states as controls? (vi) Compute the average residuals obtained in part (iv) over 1989 through 2000. Verify that you obtain the estimate in part (v) to at least six decimal places. (vii) Reproduce Table 18.6 using the southern states as the controls. Comment on similarities or important differences.
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
18  Advanced Time Series Topics

title: 18  Advanced Time Series Topics
text:
18.18 C18 [GASHOLIDAY]

Use the data in GASHOLIDAY to answer the following questions. These data were also used in Computer Exercise C15 in Chapter 10. The idea is to use gas prices in Rhode Island (RI) as a control for those in Connecticut (CT) to measure the effect of the gasoline tax holiday in CT. (i) Define the variable \(dlregCTRI = lregularCT - lregularRI\). Find the first-order autocorrelation of \(dlregCTRI\). Is it close to one? (ii) Discuss the implications of your finding in part (i) for the statistics in the regression of \(dlregCTRI\) on the dummy variable, \(holidayCT\). In particular, can you trust the \(t\) statistic and confidence interval? (iii) The regression in part (ii) is OLS applied to the equation \[dlregCTRI_t = \beta_0 + \beta_1 holidayCT_t + u_t\] If you estimate this equation by first differencing, what is the estimate of \(\beta_1\)? Does it matter much whether you include an intercept in the FD equation? How does the FD estimate compare with the estimate of \(\beta_1\) when you apply OLS in levels? (iv) Let \(\Delta holidayCT_t\) be the difference of the dummy variable. How often is this variable zero? How often is it one? What implications does this have for applying “large sample” methods of inference? (v) Referring to the material in Section 9-5c of Chapter 9, relate the regression in first differences to “outlier” analysis.
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
18  Advanced Time Series Topics

title: 19  Advanced Methods for Causal Inference
text: 19.1 C1 [JTRAIN98]
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
19  Advanced Methods for Causal Inference

title: 19  Advanced Methods for Causal Inference
text:
Consider Example 19.1, where the outcome variable, earn98, is nonnegative. Use the data in JTRAIN98. Keep all of the data without trimming for lack of overlap. (i) What is the average value of earn98? For what percent of the sample is earn98 equal to zero? (ii) Use full regression adjustment with an exponential mean and Poisson QMLE to estimate \(\tau_{ate}\), using the same controls in Example 19.1. What is \(\hat{\tau}_{ate}\), and how does it compare with linear RA in Table 19.1?



Compare the standard errors of the linear RA and exponential RA estimates. Does the exponential RA improve precision in this application?
Now use the exponential mean with IPWRA, and compare the estimate of \(\tau_{ate}\) with linear IPWRA in Table 19.1. Discuss the precision of the two estimates.
If you focus on \(\tau_{att}\) instead, does the ranking of the estimators—linear mean versus exponential mean—change?
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
19  Advanced Methods for Causal Inference

title: 19  Advanced Methods for Causal Inference
text:
19.2 C2 [VIETNAM]

Use the data in VIETNAM to answer the following questions. These are a subset of the data used in Angrist (1990). (i) The binary variable vietnam indicates that a man served the United States military during the Vietnam war. How many men are in the entire sample? How many served? (ii) Estimate a simple regress with lwage as the outcome variable and vietnam as the only explanatory variable. Interpret the estimated coefficient on vietnam. Is it statistically different from zero? (Be sure to use a heteroskedasticity-robust statistic.) (iii) Using age, educ, and black as controls, apply linear regression adjustment (with interactions) to estimate the ATE and ATT of vietnam. Do you find any effect? (iv) Estimate a logit model for vietnam using age, educ, and black as explanatory variables. Is vietnam related to any of these variables in a statistically significant way? If so, describe how. How many fitted probabilities are less than 0.1? How many are greater than 0.9. Is lack of overlap a cause for concern in this data set? (v) Using the same controls in part (iii), obtain the IPWRA estimates using a logit model for vietnam and linear models for lwage. What are the new estimates of ATE and ATT? Do they differ much from those found in part (iii)? (vi) Add nonredundant interactions among all controls to both the logit and linear models, and obtain the IPWRA estimates. What are the new estimates of ATE and ATT? Overall, what do you conclude about the causal effect of serving in Vietnam on subsequent labor market earnings?
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
19  Advanced Methods for Causal Inference

title: 19  Advanced Methods for Causal Inference
text:
19.3 C3 [JTRAIN98]

Use the variable unem98 as the outcome variable in the data set JTRAIN98. The treatment variable is train. (i) Across all men in the data set, what is the unemployment rate in 1998? How does this rate differ among those who did and did not participate in the job training program? (ii) Using the same control variables in Example 19.1—including the squares and interactions of the variables—obtain the linear RA estimates of \(\tau_{ate}\) and \(\tau_{att}\) of the effect of train on unem98. Interpret the estimated effects and obtain the \(t\) statistics. (iii) Still using the same controls, use logit regression adjustment and report \(\hat{\tau}_{ate}\) and \(\hat{\tau}_{att}\). How do these compare with the linear RA estimates? How do the standard errors of the logit RA estimates compare with the standard errors for linear RA? (iv) Use the same controls to estimate the propensity score by logit, and then obtain the IPWRA estimates of \(\tau_{ate}\) and \(\tau_{att}\) with a linear mean. How do these compare with the previous estimates? (v) Use two separate weighted regressions to obtain the fitted values in part (iv). That is, using only the controls, weight the linear regression by \(1/(1 - \hat{p}_i)\), and save the fitted values for all units. Then do the same for the treated units, where the weights are \(1/\hat{p}_i\). Difference the fitted values to obtain \(\widehat{te}_i\) for each \(i\), and then, to obtain \(\hat{\tau}_{ate}\), average the \(\widehat{te}_i\) across all \(i\). What is the estimate? Is it easy to obtain a standard error? (vi) Now obtain the IPWRA estimates where the outcome models for unem98 are also logit. Do you find any notable differences and the ATE and ATT estimates compared with part (iv)?
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
19  Advanced Methods for Causal Inference

title: 19  Advanced Methods for Causal Inference
text:
19.4 C4 [VIETNAM]

Use the data in VIETNAM for this exercise. The binary variable draft is an IV for vietnam—which indicates serving for the U.S. military during the Vietnam war. (i) Regress draft on age, educ, and black. Does it appear draft is independent of these three variables? Explain.



Estimate the first stage of 2SLS by regressing vietnam on draft, age, educ, and black. Discuss the coefficient on draft. Does draft seem suitable as an IV for vietnam?
Estimate the equation \[lwage = \alpha + \tau \cdot vietnam + \beta_1 age + \beta_2 educ + \beta_3 black + u\] by instrumental variables, using draft as an IV for vietnam. Report the coefficient on vietnam along with its standard error.
Estimate \(\tau_{late}\) using flexible equation (19.55). How large is the point estimate? What is the 95% confidence interval for \(\tau_{late}\)?
Repeat part (iii) dropping all of the control variables and obtain the IV estimate of \(\tau_{late}\). How does it compare with the LATE estimate from part (iv)? Do you think this is a more convincing estimate than those found in part (iii) or part (iv)? Explain.
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
19  Advanced Methods for Causal Inference

title: 19  Advanced Methods for Causal Inference
text:
19.5 C5 [LABSUP]

Use the data in LABSUP to answer the following questions. Use \(z = samesex\), indicating whether the first two children are born the same sex, as an instrument for \(w = morekids\), indicating having more than two children. Drop the observations where \(multi2nd = 1\) (indicating that the second birth resulted in more than one child). (i) What is the percentage of women having two children? What is the percentage having more than two children? (ii) Regress \(morekids_i\) on \(samesex_i\). What is the estimated fraction of “compliers” in the population? (iii) Use samesex as an IV for morekids in an equation with hours as the outcome variable; do not use control variables. What is the IV estimate? How does it, and its precision, compare with the OLS estimate (difference in means) and its precision? (iv) Create centered versions of the control variables age, nonmomi, and educ, about their sample averages. Use linear regression adjustment of hours on samesex, the controls, and the interactions of samesex with the centered controls. What is the coefficient on samesex? (v) Repeat part (iv), but with morekids as the dependent variable. Is the samesex coefficient the expected sign? What is its heteroskedasticity-robust \(t\) statistic? (vi) Using hours as the outcome variable and treating morekids as endogenous, use the IV estimation in (19.55) to estimate \(\tau_{late}\). Report the estimate and its standard error. (vii) Confirm that the ratio of the samesex coefficients in parts (iv) and (v) equals the IV estimate from part (vi). (viii) Estimate a probit model for morekids, in place of the linear regression in part (v). Find the average partial effect of samesex. How close is it to the coefficient on samesex in part (v)? How do they compare in terms of statistical significance? (ix) Obtain the generalized residuals from part (viii) and implement the control function approach. How does the estimate compare with the IV estimate from part (vi)? Is there evidence that morekids is endogenous? What happens to the coefficient on morekids when the two generalized residual terms are dropped? (x) Can you think of reasons why samesex might not be exogenous (satisfy Assumption LATE.2’), even after controlling for age, nonmomi, and educ?
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
19  Advanced Methods for Causal Inference

title: 19  Advanced Methods for Causal Inference
text:
19.6 C6 [RDSENATE]

Use the data in RDSENATE to answer the following questions. This is a data set on senatorial elections in the United States, similar to the data set RDHOUSE used in Example 19.3. The goal is to estimate the effect of party incumbency on election outcomes over many elections in the U.S. senate. (i) What is the calendar span of the data? How is margin measured, and what is its average value? Provide the state number and year of the smallest value of margin in absolute value. (ii) Regress Dvote on margin. What is the estimated coefficient. How do you interpret it? Is it statistically significant? (iii) Define the binary variable Dwin to be one if \(margin > 0\). (Note that this refers to the Democrat winning in the prior election, and so it acts as the “treatment” variable.) Add the > variables Dwin and \(Dwin \cdot margin\) to the regression in part (ii). What is the coefficient on Dwin? How do you interpret this coefficient? Does its magnitude seem practically important? Statistically different from zero? (iv) Do you think the interaction term \(Dwin \cdot margin\) is needed in part (iii)? What happens to the coefficient on Dwin if you drop the interaction? (v) Redo the regression in part (iii) but only use observations with \(-30 < margin < 30\). How many observations are used? What happens to the coefficient on Dwin? Is the interaction term significant? (vi) Now choose the bandwidth using the Imbens and Kalyanaraman (2012) method. What is the chosen bandwidth? What is the estimated treatment effect of party incumbency? (vii) Compare the estimates from parts (vi) and (v), and discuss the relative precision of each.
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
19  Advanced Methods for Causal Inference

title: 19  Advanced Methods for Causal Inference
text:
19.7 C7 [HEADSTART]

Use the data in HEADSTART to answer the following questions. The data are cross-sectional at the U.S. county level, and include variables from the 1960s and 1970s. Some counties were given assistance in implementing the anti-poverty program known as Head Start. The rule determining assistance turned out to be based on the poverty rate in 1960. A county received assistance if its poverty rate exceeded 59.1984. The key outcome variable is the mortality rate of young children, mort_age5_9, in years following the implementation of the national Head Start program. (i) How many counties are included in the data set? How many received assistance in setting up their Head Start program? How many counties have missing data on the variable mort_age5_9? For how many counties is mort_age5_9 equal to zero? (ii) Define the variable \(povrate\_cut = povrate - 59.1984\). Using all of the data (for which you have an observation for mort_age5_9), use OLS to estimate the equation \[mort\_age5\_9 = \beta_0 + \beta_1 assist + \beta_2 povrate\_cut + \beta_3 (assist \cdot povrate\_cut) + u.\] What is the estimated policy effect of assisting the rollout of Head Start? Is it statistically different from zero at the 5% level? (iii) In part (ii), what is the estimated coefficient on povrate_cut? Describe the intuition behind its sign. What is the estimated difference in mortality for a county with a 50 percent poverty rate and one with a 30 percent poverty rate? (iv) Redo part (ii) using only counties with \(povrate \ge 40\) and \(povrate \le 80\). How many counties are included on each side of the cutoff? How do the estimated effect of assist and its statistical significance compare with those of part (ii)? (v) Now use the Imbens and Kalyanaraman (2012) approach to choose the bandwidth. How many counties are used on either side of the cutoff? What is the estimated treatment effect (at the cutoff) and its \(t\) statistic? (vi) Starting with the same model and bandwidth as in part (iv), include controls for percent urban, percent black, and log of the population—all measured in 1960. Compare the estimated treatment effect and precision with the results found in part (iv). (vii) Use mort_age25_plus as the outcome (dependent) variable and estimate the same model, with the same bandwidth, as in part (iv). This is the mortality rate for people much too old to have participated in Head Start. Explain your findings, including why this acts as a “placebo” check on the analysis.
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
19  Advanced Methods for Causal Inference

title: 19  Advanced Methods for Causal Inference
text:
19.8 C8 [CATHOLIC]

Use the data in CATHOLIC for this question. As in Example 19.4, the goal is to try to estimate the causal effect of attending a Catholic high school on test performance. Here, when IV or CF methods are used, the binary variable, parcath, which indicates whether at least one parent is Catholic, is the single IV for cathhs, attending a Catholic high school. (i) How many students are included in this data set? How many attend a Catholic high school? In how many families is at least one parent Catholic?



Run a simple regression of cathhs on parcath (including an intercept, of course). Does the estimated coefficient make sense? Discuss its interpretation and statistical significance.
Run a simple regression of math12 on cathhs. Report and discuss the coefficient on cathhs and its statistical significance.
Now use motheduc, fatheduc, and lfaminc as controls in using (full) linear regression adjustment. What is \(\hat{\tau}_{ate}\), the estimated average treatment effect, in this case?
Regress cathhs on parcath, the controls in part (iv), and the interactions between parcath and the controls. Do it two ways: without centering the controls and with centering the controls about their sample averages. Discuss the coefficients on parcath and explain the reason for the difference. Can you conclude parcath is a suitably strong IV for cathhs?
Obtain the simple IV estimator with math12 as the outcome, cathhs as the treatment, and parcath as the IV. What is the estimate of LATE?
Now estimate LATE adding the controls in part (iv). Use equation (19.55) to allow flexible estimation. What is \(\hat{\tau}_{late}\)? Report a 95% confidence interval for \(\tau_{late}\).
Estimate a probit model for cathhs that includes parcath, the control variables in part (iv), and interactions between parcath and the centered controls. Find the average partial effect of parcath, and compare it with the appropriate estimate in part (v).
Obtain the generalized residuals from the estimation in part (viii), and implement the CF regression in equation (19.90). How does \(\hat{\tau}_{ate}\) compare with \(\hat{\tau}_{late}\) in part (vii)? (As a bonus, use bootstrapping to obtain a proper standard error for \(\hat{\tau}_{ate}\).)
In the CF regression, obtain \(\hat{\tau}_{att}\). How does it compare with \(\hat{\tau}_{ate}\)? (As a bonus, use bootstrapping to obtain a standard error for \(\hat{\tau}_{att}\).)
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
19  Advanced Methods for Causal Inference

title: 19  Advanced Methods for Causal Inference
text:
19.9 C9 [VIETNAM]

Use the data in VIETNAM for this exercise, which applies the control function method. (i) Estimate a probit model for vietnam where the explanatory variables are draft, age, educ, black, and interactions between draft and the demeaned control variables. What is the APE with respect to draft? Does it have the expected sign? What is its robust \(t\) statistic? (ii) Obtain the generalized residuals from part (i), \(gr_i\). What is the average of these residuals over \(vietnam_i = 0\)? What is the average over \(vietnam_i = 1\)? What is their average across all \(i\)? (iii) Obtain the control function estimates from the regression in (19.90). What is the estimated coefficient on vietnam? Does the effect seem practically important? If you use the usual heteroskedasticity-robust standard error, is it statistically significant? (iv) Test the null hypothesis that vietnam is exogenous in the lwage equation. What do you conclude? If you drop the terms involving \(gr_i\), what is the coefficient on vietnam? (v) Now estimate the model underlying the CF estimation by IV, where your instruments should be draft, \(draft \cdot (age - \overline{age})\), \(draft \cdot (educ - \overline{educ})\), and \(draft \cdot (black - \overline{black})\). What is the estimated effect of vietnam? What is its heteroskedasticity-robust \(t\) statistic? How does it compare with the CF estimate?
crumbs: PART 3: Advanced Topics
19  Advanced Methods for Causal Inference

title: 19  Advanced Methods for Causal Inference
text:
19.10 C10 [TUTORFRD]

Use the data in TUTORFRD to answer the following questions. These data are generated to mimic a fuzzy RD design; therefore, the findings are illustrative only. The treatment variable is tutor (participation in a summer math tutoring program between fourth and fifth grade), the running variable is score4 (the score on a fourth-grade math test), and eligibility is determined by \(score4 \le 60\). (i) How many students are in the sample? How many students were initially eligible for the tutoring program? (ii) Of the eligible students, what percentage participated? Of the ineligible students, what percentage managed to participate? (iii) Estimate a sharp RD relating tutor to elig, restricting the regression to observations with \(40 \le score4 \le 80\). How many observations are used on each side of the cutoff? What is the estimated coefficient on elig? Interpret the interaction coefficient and discuss statistical significance.



Sketch a graph similar to Figure 19.1, assuming that the tutoring program has a positive effect on score5. (Be careful; eligibility is determined here by being below the cutoff.)
Use the IV approach to estimate \(\tau_{60}\), the ATE at the cutoff \(c = 60\). Use data satisfying \(40 \le score4_i \le 80\). What is \(\hat{\tau}_{60}\) and its standard error?
Use the IK (2012) method to choose the bandwidth. What is the chosen bandwidth? How many total observations are used? How does the estimate of \(\tau_{60}\) and its precision compare with those from part (v)?
